options.A: Aqueductal stenosis
options.B: Cerebellar tonsillar herniation
options.D: Colloid cyst of the foramen of Monro
options.E: Ependymoma of the fourth ventricle
options.F: Meningioma compressing the cerebral aqueduct
options.H: Subarachnoid hemorrhage Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Aqueductal stenosis leads to dilation of the lateral and third ventricles with a small fourth ventricle, not isolated lateral ventricular enlargement. It is also a more chronic process. Choice B: Cerebellar tonsillar herniation results from severe intracranial pressure and causes brainstem compression with coma or death, but does not explain isolated ventricular enlargement patterns. Choice C: Choroid plexus papilloma increases CSF production, causing communicating hydrocephalus, typically with enlargement of all ventricles. It does not obstruct a specific foramen. Choice E: Ependymoma of the fourth ventricle causes obstruction at the level of the fourth ventricle or its outlets, resulting in dilation of the third and lateral ventricles. The fourth ventricle would be enlarged or irregular, unlike in this case. Choice F: Meningiomas are slow-growing and often asymptomatic until large. Compression of the aqueduct would cause triventricular hydrocephalus (lateral + third ventricles enlarged), not isolated lateral ventricular dilation. Choice G: Pinealomas can compress the pretectal region or the cerebral aqueduct, causing Parinaud syndrome or aqueductal obstruction. Again, they would lead to enlargement of lateral and third ventricles, not just the lateral ones. Choice H: Subarachnoid hemorrhage impairs CSF reabsorption and causes communicating hydrocephalus with global ventricular enlargement. It does not produce the obstructive pattern seen here. Bottom line: A colloid cyst of the foramen of Monro causes acute obstructive hydrocephalus with selective enlargement of the lateral ventricles, often in young adults presenting with positional headache, vomiting, and sudden collapse. Huntington disease is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by CAG trinucleotide repeat (TNR) expansion on chromosome 4. A key genetic feature is his face, arms, and legs that worsen with stress and interfere with daily activities. The anticipation, in which the number of repeats increases across successive generations, leading to earlier onset and more severe disease in children compared to parents. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C. The C7 nerve root innervates the triceps (elbow extension), wrist extensors, and provides sensation to the posterior arm and forearm, as well as the middle finger. It is the most commonly affected cervical root in radiculopathy. This patient’s combination of triceps weakness, decreased triceps reflex, wrist extensor weakness, and numbness in the third digit is classic for C7 radiculopathy, typically resulting from disc herniation or osteophyte impingement at the C6–C7 intervertebral foramen. Cervical radiculopathy frequently presents with dermatomal sensory loss, myotomal weakness, and reduced reflexes. In the case of C7, both the motor and sensory findings are highly localized and allow for accurate diagnosis without advanced imaging in many cases. The triceps reflex is a key differentiator since it is mediated by C7, while the biceps and brachioradialis reflexes are controlled by C5 and C6, respectively. Provocative maneuvers such as Spurling’s test (neck extension and rotation with axial loading) often exacerbate symptoms by narrowing the intervertebral foramina further. Management includes NSAIDs, physical therapy, and avoidance of aggravating activities. Most cases improve over time with conservative care, though persistent or progressive deficits may require MRI and neurosurgical evaluation. Recognizing the classic signs of C7 involvement—including middle finger numbness, triceps weakness, and wrist extension deficits—is crucial for early diagnosis. A 55-year-old man presents to the clinic with complaints of weakness and numbness in his right upper extremity. He works as a warehouse loader and says the symptoms began two weeks ago after lifting several heavy boxes overhead. He reports that the weakness seems to be worse when gripping objects tightly or pushing open doors. He also describes a tingling sensation that radiates from his shoulder down the posterior aspect of the arm and forearm, and into the middle finger. The symptoms worsen when he tilts his head to the right or looks upward for prolonged periods. On examination, there is decreased triceps reflex on the right compared to the left. Strength testing reveals reduced strength with wrist extension and elbow extension, though shoulder abduction and finger abduction are preserved. There is diminished pinprick sensation over the posterior forearm and dorsal surface of the third digit. Spurling’s maneuver reproduces his symptoms. X-ray of the cervical spine shows degenerative changes with disc space narrowing and osteophyte formation. Compression of which of the following spinal nerve roots most likely explains this

answer_text: T1 Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: C5 innervates the deltoid and biceps, with sensory distribution over the lateral upper arm. C5 radiculopathy causes weakness in shoulder abduction and elbow flexion, not the triceps or wrist extensors. The sensory loss would be proximal, not extending to the hand. Choice B: C6 affects the biceps, brachioradialis, and wrist extensors, and provides sensation to the lateral forearm and thumb/index fingers. C6 involvement may include reduced biceps or brachioradialis reflexes and lateral hand paresthesias, but it spares the middle finger and triceps. Choice D: C8 controls finger flexors and intrinsic hand muscles, with sensory distribution over the medial forearm and fourth/fifth digits. Weakness would be seen in grip strength and finger flexion, not wrist extension or triceps strength. This patient's distribution does not match C8. Choice E: T1 innervates the intrinsic hand muscles, including those responsible for finger abduction and fine motor control. T1 radiculopathy causes sensory loss over the medial arm and may cause difficulty with hand coordination, but not triceps weakness or middle finger numbness. Bottom line: C7 radiculopathy presents with triceps weakness, diminished triceps reflex, wrist extensor weakness, and numbness in the middle finger. It is typically due to foraminal narrowing at the C6–C7 level, and most cases improve with conservative therapy. Choice B: Autonomic storm (or sympathetic storm) can occur after traumatic brain injury, but it is characterized by fever, hypertension, diaphoresis, and tachycardia due to excessive sympathetic discharge. This patient, by contrast, has bradycardia, which is not consistent with a sympathetic storm. The triad present is more specific for a reflex response to intracranial vascular compression rather than generalized sympathetic hyperactivity. Choice D: Direct cardiac injury can cause arrhythmias, including sinus node dysfunction, but it does not produce the simultaneous hypertension and depressed respirations observed here. Additionally, there were no signs of thoracic trauma in this patient — no chest wall bruising, abnormal heart sounds, or pulmonary findings. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C. This is a lower motor neuron (LMN) facial nerve palsy. LMN lesions of CN VII result in weakness of both the upper and lower facial muscles on the affected side, including the forehead. The absence of systemic findings or vesicles supports idiopathic inflammatory neuropathy (post-viral), the most common cause of LMN facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). The nerve is affected after it exits the brainstem, distal to the facial nucleus. A 42-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset weakness of the right side of her face. She is unable to close her right eye, raise her right eyebrow, or smile symmetrically. She reports recent recovery from an upper respiratory illness. She denies rash, ear pain, and tick exposure. Neurologic examination reveals complete right-sided facial paralysis. Strength and sensation in the limbs are normal. Which of the following best explains the underlying cause of her condition? A. Infarction of corticobulbar fibers in the left internal capsule

stem: Choice B: The median nerve (C5–T1) travels with the brachial artery in the arm, passes between the pronator teres heads, and enters the hand via the carpal tunnel. It supplies motor innervation to most forearm flexors and the thenar muscles via the recurrent branch (opponens pollicis, abductor pollicis brevis, flexor pollicis brevis), and provides sensory input to the lateral palm and palmar aspects of the lateral 3½ digits (plus dorsal distal tips). Injury leads to thenar weakness, loss of thumb opposition, and sensory deficits; carpal tunnel compression causes nocturnal paresthesias and thenar atrophy. Because it contains critical motor fibers for hand function, a biopsy here risks disabling motor impairment and is inappropriate when aiming to avoid motor dysfunction.
answer_text: Median nerve

options.E: Ulnar nerve Cutaneous nerves of the hand. Choice C: The radial nerve (C5–T1) courses in the spiral groove with the profunda brachii artery, innervates the triceps, then splits into deep (posterior interosseous) and superficial branches near the lateral epicondyle. It is the primary motor supply to wrist and finger extensors, with sensory coverage to the posterior arm/forearm and dorsal lateral hand. Injury produces the HY finding of wrist drop and loss of finger extension, often with dorsal hand numbness; compression can occur with prolonged pressure (“Saturday night palsy”). Because biopsy would endanger essential motor function for wrist and finger extension, this nerve is not selected when preserving motor strength is a priority. Choice E: The ulnar nerve (C8–T1) passes posterior to the medial epicondyle (funny bone), enters the forearm between the FCU heads, and reaches the hand via Guyon’s canal. It supplies motor fibers to the intrinsic hand muscles (interossei, medial lumbricals) and forearm flexors (FCU and medial FDP) and provides sensory innervation to the medial 1½ digits and corresponding hand regions, palmar and dorsal. Lesions cause grip weakness, loss of finger abduction/adduction, and ulnar claw posture with sensory loss along the medial hand. Because of its significant motor role in intrinsic hand function, ulnar nerve biopsy risks permanent motor deficits and is not appropriate when motor preservation is required. Bottom line: The sural nerve is a purely sensory nerve located along the posterior-lateral leg and lateral foot. Its lack of motor fibers allows biopsy without causing muscle weakness, making it the preferred site when histopathologic confirmation of a neuropathy is required. This approach preserves motor function while still allowing for definitive diagnosis of conditions such as vasculitis, amyloidosis, leprosy, or hereditary neuropathies. Choice C: Myasthenic neuromuscular junction disorders, such as myasthenia gravis, classically present with fluctuating muscle weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. Ocular symptoms (ptosis and diplopia) are common, but complete hemifacial paralysis involving both the upper and lower face is not typical. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is A. This patient has impaired thermoregulation due to anterior hypothalamic damage. The anterior hypothalamus controls heat dissipation (“A/C center”) by promoting vasodilation and sweating during hyperthermia. Damage to this region impairs the body’s ability to cool itself, leading to hyperthermia, flushed skin, and autonomic instability. The anterior hypothalamus receives input from thermoreceptors and coordinates parasympathetic responses to dissipate heat. The posterior hypothalamus, by contrast, promotes heat conservation. The hypothalamic nuclei can be remembered with a simple "animal farm" style mnemonic. The preoptic nucleus ("front of the eyes — eyes show lust") regulates GnRH secretion. The supraoptic nucleus ("above the eyes — lacrimal glands above eyes secrete tears") controls water balance via ADH secretion. The suprachiasmatic nucleus ("the eyes close during sleep") controls the circadian rhythm. The anterior nucleus ("wet nose — cooling system in animals") controls body cooling and parasympathetic activity. The lateral nucleus ("hunger center — animal eats"), when stimulated, increases hunger; while the ventromedial nucleus (VMN) ("satiety center — stops eating") promotes satiety. The dorsomedial nucleus (DMN) helps regulate gastrointestinal stimulation. The arcuate nucleus ("curved udder of a cow") produces hypothalamic releasing hormones including dopamine, which inhibits prolactin and milk secretion. The posterior nucleus ("farting posterior of a cow") controls heating and sympathetic tone. Finally, the mammillary bodies ("mammary = memory") are involved in memory processing as part of the Papez circuit, receiving input from the hippocampus via the fornix and projecting to the anterior thalamus via the mammillothalamic tract. A 33-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after collapsing during a marathon on a hot summer day. On arrival, his temperature is 39.5°C (103.1°F), and he appears flushed and confused. He is diaphoretic with hypotension and tachycardia. Neurologic exam is otherwise normal. Further evaluation reveals impaired heat dissipation and autonomic dysregulation. MRI shows a small lesion affecting part of the hypothalamus. Which of the following areas is most likely involved?

answer_text: Lateral hypothalamus

options.A: Anterior hypothalamus
options.B: Arcuate nucleus
options.E: Suprachiasmatic nucleus Wrong answer explanations: Choice B: (Arcuate nucleus): Involved in appetite regulation and neuroendocrine control (dopamine, GnRH release), not temperature regulation. Choice C: (Lateral hypothalamus): Stimulates hunger; lesions here cause anorexia and weight loss. Choice D: (Posterior hypothalamus): Responsible for heat conservation; lesions here would cause hypothermia. Choice E: (Suprachiasmatic nucleus): Regulates circadian rhythms via light input; not involved in thermoregulation. Bottom line: Anterior hypothalamus = heat dissipation (vasodilation, sweating) → lesion → hyperthermia. Anatomy Neurology (Normal structure and function of the nervous system) None

stem: Choice C: Multiple sclerosis typically presents in young women (ages 20–40) with relapsing-remitting neurological deficits separated in time and space. While spasticity and incontinence can occur, bilateral leg symptoms without upper limb involvement or sensory loss would be unusual. Also, MS does not cause increased intracranial pressure or papilledema. The progressive course and age make MS highly unlikely. The gait is characterized by short, shuffling steps and difficulty initiating movement, as if the feet are stuck to the floor (magnetic gait). This differs from the spastic, scissoring gait seen in bilateral upper motor neuron lesions. Although NPH can lead to frontal lobe dysfunction and incontinence, it does not cause morning headaches, nausea, or papilledema because intracranial pressure is not elevated. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C. This patient has a right cerebellar hemispheric lesion. The cerebellar hemispheres control coordination of the ipsilateral limbs, leading to right-sided dysmetria, intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia, and falling toward the right. Limb ataxia is the hallmark of hemispheric lesions. In contrast, lesions of the cerebellar vermis primarily affect midline structures, leading to truncal ataxia, wide-based gait, and balance difficulty without limb-specific dysmetria. Because the cerebellum controls the same side of the body, the lesion is localized ipsilaterally. A 62-year-old man presents with unsteady gait and tremor. On exam, he has difficulty performing finger-to-nose testing on the right side, with overshooting of his hand (dysmetria). He also has intention tremor, dysdiadochokinesia on the right, and falls toward the right when standing. His gait is otherwise wide-based. There is no limb weakness or sensory loss. They are not typically calcified with cystic components or associated with bitemporal hemianopsia. However, they typically do not show calcification or cystic degeneration on imaging and are not associated with pituitary hormone deficiencies. Choice D: Pinealomas are tumors of the pineal region that can cause Parinaud syndrome (upward gaze palsy, light-near dissociation, and convergence-retraction nystagmus) by compressing the tectal plate. They may also present with hydrocephalus due to aqueductal stenosis. These tumors do not cause bitemporal hemianopsia.

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is E. This patient demonstrates a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD; aka Marcus-Gunn pupil) in the right eye, a classic early finding in optic neuritis, one of the most common visual manifestations of multiple sclerosis. In the swinging flashlight test, both pupils constrict when light is shown in the left eye (normal afferent input), but paradoxically dilate when the light is shown in the right eye. This is because the right optic nerve fails to deliver adequate afferent input. As a result, the brainstem generates a weaker parasympathetic efferent response, and both pupils constrict less. To understand this, recall the normal pupillary light reflex: light enters the eye and travels via the optic nerve (CN II) to the pretectal nucleus, which activates both Edinger-Westphal nuclei. These then send parasympathetic fibers via the oculomotor nerves (CN III) to the ciliary ganglia and onward to both pupillary constrictor muscles. When the afferent limb is impaired, the entire reflex is weakened, despite intact efferent output. A 27-year-old graphic designer comes to the clinic with a 5-day history of visual blurring in her right eye. She was diagnosed with relapsing-remitting multiple sclerosis two years ago. She reports difficulty seeing fine details, especially in bright environments. On examination, both pupils are 6 mm in darkness and 4 mm in ambient light. When light is directed into the left eye, both pupils constrict to 2 mm. When the light is moved to the right eye, both pupils return to 4 mm. Extraocular movements are intact. No ptosis is noted. Which of the following structures is most likely affected?

stem: Subject System Topic
answer_text: Right optic nerve

options.A: Left ciliary ganglion
options.B: Left oculomotor nerve
options.E: Right optic nerve
options.F: Visual cortex Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: The ciliary ganglion relays parasympathetic fibers from the oculomotor nerve to the sphincter pupillae, enabling pupillary constriction. A lesion here would result in a dilated, poorly reactive pupil on the same side, due to disruption of the efferent pathway. However, this patient has normal constriction when light is directed into the left eye, indicating that the left ciliary ganglion is functioning properly. The paradoxical dilation when light is moved to the right eye suggests a right-sided afferent defect, not an efferent issue on the left. Choice B: The oculomotor nerve carries motor fibers for extraocular muscles and parasympathetic fibers to the pupil. Damage would cause ptosis, "down and out" eye deviation, and a fixed, dilated pupil on the affected side. None of these findings are present on physical exam. Additionally, the left pupil constricts normally to light, confirming that the left oculomotor nerve is intact. Choice C: The optic tract carries post-chiasmal visual input from both eyes and lesions here usually produce contralateral homonymous hemianopia. Although severe optic tract lesions can occasionally cause an RAPD, it would be on the side opposite the lesion. In this case, light shown into the left eye produces a normal response, and the defect is clearly right-sided, making a left optic tract lesion unlikely. Choice D: The Edinger-Westphal nucleus sends parasympathetic output through the oculomotor nerve to mediate pupillary constriction. A lesion here would impair constriction of the right pupil, but the left pupil would still constrict when either eye is stimulated. In this case, both pupils fail to constrict when light is shown into the right eye, which is consistent with impaired afferent input, not efferent output. The right pupil also constricts when light is shown in the left eye, which further rules out an efferent lesion. Choice F: The visual cortex processes visual perception but is not involved in the pupillary light reflex, which is a subcortical reflex mediated through the midbrain. Damage here would result in loss of conscious visual awareness in specific visual fields, but the pupillary response to light would remain intact. Since the reflex is mediated by pretectal and midbrain nuclei before reaching the cortex, lesions in the visual cortex cannot explain the absent constriction with right-eye stimulation. The findings are more consistent with an afferent visual pathway defect. High Yield USMLE Audio Answer: Cut to the chase Audio Answer: Bottom line: Optic neuritis is classically seen in MS on USMLE. It often presents as a RAPD, where attenuated afferent input from CN II results in apparent dilation of the pupils due to reciprocally lesser CN III parasympathetic efferent output. Physiology Neurology (Demyelinating diseases) None

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is E. Strabismus is characterized by misalignment of the eyes due to dysfunction of extraocular muscles or their innervation, leading to one eye deviating inward (esotropia) or outward (exotropia) relative to the other. In children, this results in impaired binocular vision, loss of depth perception, and can cause diplopia (double vision) as the brain receives two non-fused images. If untreated, the brain suppresses the image from the misaligned eye, predisposing to amblyopia—a loss of visual acuity in the affected eye due to cortical adaptation. A 6-year-old girl is brought to the clinic by her father due to concerns about her vision. The child often tilts her head when trying to read and has trouble catching objects during play. She occasionally complains of seeing double, especially when focusing on things up close. Her past medical history is unremarkable, and there is no family history of eye disease. On ophthalmologic examination, her right eye is noted to deviate inward upon near gaze, while her left eye appears to fixate normally. Visual acuity is normal in both eyes, but the cover-uncover test demonstrates shifting fixation between the eyes. The remainder of her neurological examination is within normal limits. Which of the following best explains the underlying pathology responsible for this patient's presentation?

stem: Pathophysiologically, strabismus arises from either an imbalance in extraocular muscle strength, abnormal cranial nerve function (commonly CN III, IV, or VI), or congenital defects in ocular muscle anatomy. The clinical hallmark is ocular misalignment, which can present as esotropia, exotropia, hypertropia, or hypotropia. Children may develop
answer_text: Strabismus the primary cause of ocular misalignment or diplopia; rather, it is a potential complication of untreated strabismus. Clinically, amblyopia presents as unilateral or bilateral decreased vision without a structural abnormality of the eye, and children typically do not have diplopia since the brain suppresses the weaker image. Treatment focuses on addressing the underlying cause (e.g., patching the dominant eye) to force use of the amblyopic eye and stimulate visual development. Choice B: Astigmatism is caused by an irregular curvature of the cornea or lens, resulting in distorted or blurred vision at all distances. The abnormal shape leads to light focusing at multiple points on the retina, compromising image clarity. This condition does not cause eye misalignment, diplopia, or abnormal cover-uncover test findings. Astigmatism commonly presents with visual blurring, headaches, and eyestrain, but does not produce diplopia or strabismus. It is corrected with cylindrical lenses that compensate for the irregular refractive power of the cornea or lens. Choice C: Hyperopia (farsightedness) occurs when the eyeball is too short anteroposteriorly or the cornea has insufficient curvature, causing light to focus behind the retina. Patients with hyperopia see distant objects more clearly than near objects, often leading to difficulty with close work. Children may compensate with increased accommodation, but this can cause eyestrain or headaches. Hyperopia does not cause ocular misalignment or diplopia, but uncorrected severe hyperopia can predispose to accommodative esotropia, a specific form of strabismus, which may overlap with this presentation but is not the general mechanism described here. Clinical presentation is typically blurry near vision, not double vision or shifting ocular fixation. Choice D: Myopia (nearsightedness) is due to an elongated eyeball or excessive corneal curvature, resulting in light focusing in front of the retina. This causes distant objects to appear blurry while near vision remains clear. Myopia does not cause strabismus or issues with binocular alignment, and patients do not exhibit abnormal findings on cover- uncover testing. Severe myopia increases the risk of retinal detachment due to elongation of the globe, but does not account for the described diplopia or misalignment. Clinical features center around blurry distance vision and frequent squinting, not double vision or depth perception loss. Bottom line: Strabismus is a disorder of ocular alignment that results in binocular vision loss, diplopia, and impaired depth perception, and can cause amblyopia if untreated. Differentiating strabismus from refractive errors like astigmatism, hyperopia, and myopia is critical, as only strabismus presents with eye misalignment and abnormal cover-uncover test. stem: Choice B: Rupture of bridging cortical veins leads to subdural hematoma, commonly seen in elderly patients following minor trauma or in shaken infants. Subdural hematoma presents as a crescent-shaped bleed on CT and features a slower onset of symptoms such as headache and confusion, without acute third nerve palsy or a dilated, non-reactive pupil. There is also no classic association with neck stiffness from meningeal irritation in subdural hematoma. Choice C: The middle cerebral artery (MCA) is a common site for ischemic stroke and large vessel infarcts, which present with contralateral hemiparesis, hemisensory loss (face and arm > leg), and aphasia (if dominant hemisphere involved). MCA aneurysms do exist but rarely cause isolated cranial nerve palsies; they more often produce focal
answer_text: Posterior communicating artery Anatomy Neurology (Strokes & Stroke-related stuff) None Subject System Topic

Epidural hematoma presents with a classic lucid interval followed by rapid neurologic deterioration due to increased intracranial pressure, but does not cause isolated cranial nerve III palsy or meningeal signs. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is E. This patient has hemiballismus, characterized by violent, flinging movements of the contralateral limbs. Hemiballismus results from a lesion of the subthalamic nucleus, part of the indirect pathway of the basal ganglia that normally suppresses movement. Loss of subthalamic inhibition leads to excessive movement. Because the symptoms affect the left limbs, the lesion must be in the right subthalamic nucleus. Small lacunar strokes from hypertensive microvascular disease are the most common cause. A 55-year-old woman presents with involuntary movements of her left arm and leg that started suddenly. On exam, she has large, flinging, ballistic movements of the left upper and lower extremity. Muscle strength, tone, and sensation are otherwise normal. MRI of the brain shows a small lacunar infarct. Which of the following areas is most likely affected?

options.E: S1–S2 Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: L1–L2 contributes to hip flexion and provides sensory innervation to the inguinal region and upper thigh. Lesions at this level may cause groin pain and difficulty flexing the hip but would not produce knee extension weakness or patellar reflex changes. Choice C: L4–L5 radiculopathy typically causes dorsiflexion weakness and sensory loss over the lateral leg and dorsum of the foot. The reflexes remain intact, and knee extension is usually spared. This patient's symptoms are more proximal and don't match the sensory distribution of L4–L5. Choice D: L5–S1 radiculopathy results in plantarflexion weakness, absent ankle reflex, and posterior leg and lateral foot sensory loss. It does not affect the quadriceps or patellar reflex. Gait disturbances such as toe-walking are more common with S1 involvement. Choice E: S1–S2 primarily involves muscles responsible for ankle plantarflexion and foot intrinsic movement. Sensory changes affect the posterior leg and foot, not the anterior thigh or medial leg. The knee extension strength and patellar reflex would be normal in these lesions. Bottom line: L3–L4 radiculopathy causes anterior thigh pain, knee extension weakness, medial leg sensory loss, and diminished patellar reflex. It is often due to disc herniation or foraminal narrowing at the L3–L4 level and should be considered in patients with proximal leg symptoms and preserved foot strength. Choice C: Anticoagulant therapy increases the risk of intracerebral hemorrhage in general. However, there is no mention of this patient being on warfarin or direct oral anticoagulants. It can be associated with acute endocarditis, which in theory can lead to a septic embolus to the brain (i.e., a type of stroke), but there's no reason to assume this patient is an IV drug user. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C. Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), also known as pseudotumor cerebri, is characterized by increased intracranial pressure (ICP) without radiographic evidence of mass lesion or hydrocephalus and with normal cerebrospinal fluid composition. It classically affects overweight women of childbearing age and presents with headache, transient visual disturbances (including diplopia due to cranial nerve VI palsy), papilledema, and sometimes pulsatile tinnitus. A 31-year-old woman presents with progressively worsening headaches, nausea, and transient blurred vision for 1 week. She reports recent weight gain and has a BMI of 37. Her blood pressure is 146/92 mmHg, and pulse is 57/min. On physical examination, she has bilateral sixth nerve palsy and visual obscurations with bending over. Fundoscopic examination reveals papilledema and loss of spontaneous venous pulsations. Non-contrast CT scan of the head reveals no masses, hemorrhage, or hydrocephalus. A lumbar puncture is performed, showing an opening pressure of 315 mmH2O, with normal CSF composition. She is not currently taking any medications, including oral contraceptives or vitamin supplements. Which of the following is the most likely explanation for her symptoms?

stem: Pathophysiologically, IIH is thought to result from impaired CSF absorption at the arachnoid villi, leading to increased ICP. Risk factors include obesity, female sex, and, less commonly, use of medications such as vitamin A, tetracyclines, and oral contraceptives. Classic findings include papilledema, absent venous pulsations on fundoscopy, and an elevated opening pressure on lumbar puncture with otherwise normal CSF analysis. The diagnosis is clinical and supported by neuroimaging to exclude secondary causes; a normal CT/MRI in the presence of elevated opening pressure is characteristic.
answer_text: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension Choice A: Acetazolamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor used to treat IIH by reducing CSF production. Its adverse effects include metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia, renal stones, and paresthesia, but it is not associated with causing increased intracranial pressure or the presenting syndrome. The patient is not taking this medication, nor is there

options.A: Adverse effect of acetazolamide First-line treatment is acetazolamide, a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that decreases CSF production, with therapeutic lumbar punctures and weight loss also beneficial.
options.B: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis Wrong answer explanations:
options.C: Idiopathic intracranial hypertension Choice A: Acetazolamide is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor used to treat IIH by reducing CSF production. Its adverse effects include metabolic acidosis, hypokalemia, renal stones, and paresthesia, but it is not associated with causing increased intracranial pressure or the presenting syndrome. The patient is not taking this medication, nor is there
options.D: Meningioma compressing the optic nerve evidence of its typical side effects.
options.E: Normal pressure hydrocephalus Choice B: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST) may present with headache, papilledema, and signs of increased ICP. However, CVST often occurs in the context of hypercoagulable states, peripartum period, infection, or malignancy, and neuroimaging would typically reveal indirect signs such as empty delta sign, hemorrhagic infarcts, or venous filling defects. This patient’s imaging is normal and there is no mention of risk factors like recent pregnancy, infection, or thrombophilia. Choice D: Meningiomas are slow-growing extra-axial tumors, often presenting with focal neurological deficits or chronic progressive visual loss if compressing the optic nerve. They typically show enhancing masses on imaging, and are not associated with acutely elevated ICP in the absence of mass effect. Classic presentation involves gradual monocular vision loss and proptosis, not the bilateral papilledema and normal imaging seen here. Choice E: Normal pressure hydrocephalus (NPH) occurs in individuals over 60, presenting with the classic triad of urinary incontinence, gait disturbance (“magnetic gait”), and cognitive impairment (“wet, wobbly, wacky”). Imaging reveals enlarged ventricles out of proportion to sulcal atrophy. NPH does not cause papilledema or elevated opening pressure on lumbar puncture, and is not seen in young adults. Bottom line: IIH is a diagnosis of exclusion seen in overweight women of childbearing age with signs of increased intracranial pressure (headache, papilledema, cranial nerve palsies) and normal neuroimaging except for possible empty sella. The hallmark is elevated opening pressure on lumbar puncture with normal CSF studies. Pathology Neurology (Headache) None Subject System Topic

This patient presents with classic features of a radial nerve injury, most commonly associated with midshaft humeral fractures, especially those involving the radial groove. The radial nerve innervates the extensors of the wrist, fingers, and elbow. Injury at this level disrupts motor control of the extensors, leading to the hallmark sign: wrist drop, where the hand hangs in a pronated, flexed position due to unopposed flexor tone. Additionally, sensation over the dorsal hand is provided by the superficial branch of the radial nerve, which explains the patient's sensory loss. stem: Choice B: The axillary nerve innervates the deltoid and is responsible for shoulder abduction between 15–90 degrees, with sensory coverage over the lateral upper arm. Injury usually occurs from shoulder dislocation or surgical neck fractures. This patient has intact shoulder motion and no sensory loss in the deltoid region. The radial nerve, not axillary, passes through the radial groove, which aligns with the described fracture pattern. Label B is anatomically correct for axillary nerve, but clinically inconsistent. Choice D: The median nerve innervates the thenar muscles and supplies sensation to the lateral 3½ digits of the hand. Median nerve injury presents with thenar weakness, impaired thumb opposition, and sensory loss in the palmar hand. This patient shows no thenar atrophy, preserved thumb opposition, and the sensory deficit is dorsal, not palmar. Additionally, median nerve palsy is associated with supracondylar fractures or carpal tunnel, not midshaft humeral injuries. Label D identifies the median nerve correctly, but it's not the cause here. Choice E: The ulnar nerve innervates the medial 1½ digits, the interosseous muscles, and the hypothenar muscles. Ulnar nerve injuries present with claw hand, grip weakness, and Froment sign (failure of thumb adduction). This patient has no hypothenar atrophy, normal finger adduction, and sensory loss in the dorsal, not medial palmar, hand.

Classic findings include wrist drop, weak grip, and sensory loss over the dorsum of the hand; elbow flexion is preserved if the lesion is distal to the triceps branch. Choice C: Takayasu arteritis is a granulomatous large-vessel vasculitis that primarily affects young women under 40 years of age, often of Asian descent. Unlike temporal arteritis, Takayasu arteritis does not classically involve the temporal artery or present with headaches and jaw claudication. Constitutional symptoms can occur in both diseases, but Takayasu arteritis causes arterial occlusive symptoms such as pulselessness rather than scalp tenderness and vision loss. Choice B: This motor nerve innervates the gluteus maximus, which functions in hip extension, such as rising from a seated position or climbing stairs. Injury presents as difficulty extending the thigh, but no sensory loss. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C This patient has right lateral pontine syndrome, caused by infarction of the anterior inferior cerebellar artery (AICA), which supplies the lateral pons. The most important localizing sign is ipsilateral facial weakness (Bell’s palsy) due to involvement of the facial nucleus (CN VII). Facial weakness is the key clinical clue that helps distinguish lateral pontine syndrome from lateral medullary syndrome. Involvement of the vestibular nuclei causes vertigo and imbalance, while cochlear nuclei damage produces hearing loss. The spinal trigeminal nucleus is involved, leading to ipsilateral facial sensory loss. Injury to the spinothalamic tract causes contralateral body pain and temperature loss. The middle cerebellar peduncle is affected as well, leading to ipsilateral limb ataxia and falling to the right. The mnemonic “FACIAL” helps recall that the Face is affected, and AICA is embedded backwards inside the word FACIAL. A 61-year-old man presents after sudden onset of vertigo, nausea, and unsteady gait. On neurologic exam, he has right-sided facial weakness, decreased sensation over the right side of his face, hearing loss in the right ear, and unsteady gait with falling toward the right. He also has decreased pain and temperature sensation on the left side of his body. Proprioception, strength, and extraocular movements are normal. MRI shows a small infarct in the pons. Which of the following areas is most likely involved?
learning_summary: The correct answer is C This patient has right lateral pontine syndrome, caused by infarction of the anterior inferior cerebellar artery (AICA), which supplies the lateral pons

stem: Subject System Topic
answer_text: Right medial pons

options.A: Left lateral pons
options.B: Left medial pons
options.E: Right midbrain Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: (Left lateral pons): Would produce facial weakness, hearing loss, and facial sensory loss on the left, which does not match this patient. Choice B: (Left medial pons): Would cause right-sided body weakness with left abducens palsy, but not facial weakness or hearing loss. Choice D: (Right medial pons): Would cause left-sided body weakness with right abducens palsy, not Bell’s palsy or hearing loss. Choice E: (Right midbrain): Midbrain lesions primarily affect CN III, causing ptosis and down-and-out gaze; facial nerve and hearing are unaffected. Bottom line: Lateral pontine syndrome = ipsilateral Bell’s palsy ± hearing loss → AICA infarct → lateral pons → remember: FACIAL = AICA. Anatomy Neurology (Strokes & Stroke-related stuff) None

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is D. This patient has a winged scapula, evidenced by the medial border of the scapula lifting away from the thoracic wall when pushing against resistance. This is due to paralysis of the serratus anterior muscle, which anchors the scapula to the chest wall and facilitates upward rotation necessary for full overhead abduction. The long thoracic nerve (C5–C7), which innervates the serratus anterior, is uniquely susceptible to injury because it courses superficially along the lateral thoracic wall. It can be injured by repetitive trauma, surgical procedures (e.g., mastectomy), or activities involving prolonged or forceful shoulder movement, such as boxing or weightlifting. Because the long thoracic nerve is a pure motor nerve, sensation is preserved, as in this case. Other shoulder muscles such as the deltoid or rotator cuff may compensate partially, but patients still notice weakness or discomfort with overhead movements. Winging is accentuated when patients perform a wall push test. Management is typically conservative, with physical therapy; severe or persistent cases may require surgical intervention. A 26-year-old man comes to the clinic with difficulty lifting his left arm above his head and worsening shoulder discomfort. He is a boxer and reports that the symptoms began gradually after increasing his training intensity. He has no history of trauma, cervical spine symptoms, or prior surgeries. On physical examination, there is prominence of the left scapula when he is asked to push against the wall with outstretched arms. Strength testing reveals preserved shoulder abduction and normal sensation in the upper extremity. There is no muscle wasting or fasciculations. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured in this patient?

Injury causes weakness in those movements but does not result in scapular winging. It may occur in sports with overhead motion but wouldn’t produce the wall push test findings seen here. Bottom line: Long thoracic nerve injury causes medial scapular winging due to paralysis of the serratus anterior, particularly noticeable when pushing against a wall. explanation: The correct answer is A. This patient presents with clinical findings consistent with axillary nerve injury, most likely due to a fracture of the surgical neck of the humerus. The axillary nerve arises from the C5–C6 nerve roots and innervates the deltoid muscle, which is responsible for shoulder abduction between 15–90 degrees. The patient’s inability to lift the arm beyond the initial range of motion, along with flattening of the shoulder contour, strongly suggests deltoid muscle dysfunction caused by axillary nerve damage. In addition, the decreased sensation over the lateral upper arm supports involvement of the axillary nerve, which provides cutaneous innervation to the skin overlying the deltoid region. options.A: Common peroneal (fibular) nerve
options.B: Femoral nerve
options.E: Tibial nerve Lower limb nerves. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: The common peroneal nerve is vulnerable to compression at the fibular neck and causes motor deficits in both dorsiflexion and eversion, leading to foot drop and high-steppage gait. Sensory loss typically affects the lateral leg and dorsum of the foot, not the medial calf. This patient has preserved gait, strength, and no sensory disturbance in the lateral distribution. Moreover, the position described (leg crossing) would be more likely to affect superficial medial structures than this deeper nerve behind the fibular head. The absence of foot involvement further argues against common peroneal injury. Choice B: The femoral nerve innervates the anterior thigh muscles (quadriceps) and is responsible for knee extension and hip flexion, along with sensory input to the anterior thigh and medial leg. However, femoral nerve injury typically presents with weakness in hip flexion and knee extension, often accompanied by a diminished patellar reflex. This patient has normal motor strength and reflexes, making injury to the entire femoral nerve unlikely. Additionally, there is no anterior thigh involvement. A focal sensory-only deficit is better explained by a distal branch of the femoral nerve rather than the nerve trunk itself. Choice C: The obturator nerve provides motor innervation to the medial thigh muscles (adductors) and sensory innervation to the medial thigh. Injury leads to adduction weakness, unsteady gait, or medial thigh numbness. This patient has no motor weakness, no gait abnormality, and no sensory deficit in the thigh, making obturator nerve injury an incorrect fit. The medial calf territory lies beyond the sensory distribution of the obturator nerve. There is also no history of pelvic trauma or surgery to suggest obturator involvement. Choice E: The tibial nerve supplies the posterior compartment of the leg, enabling plantarflexion and inversion, and carries sensation to the sole of the foot and heel. Injury results in difficulty walking, especially during push-off, and may present with foot pain or numbness on the plantar surface. This patient has intact motor strength, no difficulty with walking or toe-stands, and normal plantar sensation, ruling out tibial involvement. The sensory loss is localized to the medial calf, which is outside the tibial nerve territory. Bottom line: The saphenous nerve is a pure sensory branch of the femoral nerve, supplying the medial lower leg. It is commonly injured by compression or traction, such as during prolonged leg crossing or kneeling. Injury causes isolated sensory loss over the medial calf with no motor or reflex abnormalities. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is D. Although the patient perceives “loss of taste,” flavor depends far more on olfaction than on the tongue’s gustatory pathways. The olfactory nerve (cranial nerve I) transmits sensory fibers from the nasal epithelium through the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone to the olfactory bulb. In head trauma such as a car accident, shearing forces can sever these delicate fibers as they cross the cribriform plate, leading to anosmia or hyposmia. When smell is impaired, patients experience food as bland or tasteless even though the taste buds innervated by cranial nerves VII, IX, and X remain intact. This patient’s car accident provides a clear mechanism for olfactory nerve injury. The fact that he has an otherwise unremarkable neurologic and physical examination, implying normal facial movement, normal tongue motion, and intact swallowing, argues strongly against facial, glossopharyngeal, hypoglossal, or vagus nerve lesions. True isolated taste A 44-year-old man comes to the clinic because food has tasted bland for the past 2 loss from gustatory nerve dysfunction is rare, while olfactory nerve injury from trauma is a well-established cause of this symptom. months, ever since he was in a minor car accident in which his head struck the steering wheel. He did not lose consciousness at the time. The rest of the neurologic

options.A: Facial nerve Choice C: The hypoglossal nerve is a pure motor nerve responsible for controlling movements of the tongue. Damage produces weakness of the tongue, which deviates toward the affected side on protrusion, and may also cause articulation problems or difficulty manipulating food in the mouth. Importantly, the hypoglossal nerve has no role in taste sensation whatsoever. Patients with hypoglossal lesions typically report dysarthria and difficulty chewing or swallowing, rather than bland taste. This patient’s tongue examination is normal, with symmetric motion and no deviation. Speech is clear, and there are no signs of lingual weakness. Because taste is not mediated by cranial nerve XII, and because
options.B: Glossopharyngeal nerve his tongue motor function is intact, hypoglossal nerve injury is excluded in this case.
options.C: Hypoglossal nerve Choice E: The vagus nerve provides limited taste sensation from the epiglottis and pharynx and also carries motor fibers to the larynx, pharynx, and soft palate. Lesions in this nerve typically present with hoarseness (due to vocal cord paralysis), dysphagia (from pharyngeal weakness), and impaired gag reflex. Patients may also demonstrate deviation of the uvula away from the side of the lesion because of palatal weakness. In severe cases, aspiration and nasal regurgitation can occur. Isolated taste complaints would not be expected, since vagus nerve dysfunction nearly always produces motor deficits in the pharynx and larynx. In this patient, there are no swallowing problems, no voice changes, and
options.E: Vagus nerve Bottom line: Although this patient reports “loss of taste,” the underlying problem is almost always olfactory nerve dysfunction, especially after trauma involving the cribriform plate, which can shear olfactory fibers. By contrast, facial nerve lesions rarely cause isolated taste loss, as they typically present with Bell’s palsy and sometimes hyperacusis, making the olfactory nerve the most likely culprit in this case. The pathologic hallmark is the degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, leading to reduced dopamine levels in the striatum. In this vignette, however, the resting tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, and stooped gait result from dopaminergic neuron degeneration, not cholinergic deficiency. Choice C: GABA is the primary inhibitory neurotransmitter in the CNS, released by interneurons and striatal medium spiny neurons. Its loss is most notable in Huntington disease, an autosomal dominant CAG repeat disorder that causes caudate atrophy, chorea, psychiatric disturbance, and eventual dementia. Decreased GABA and acetylcholine with increased dopamine in the basal ganglia lead to hyperkinetic movements. Clinically, drugs that enhance GABA signaling, such as benzodiazepines, barbiturates, and baclofen, are used to treat anxiety, epilepsy, and spasticity. Huntington disease, however, is distinct from the parkinsonian syndrome described here: it begins with hyperkinesia and psychiatric changes rather than bradykinesia and rigidity, and the neurotransmitter primarily deficient in Parkinson disease is dopamine. Choice D: Glutamate is the main excitatory neurotransmitter in the CNS, essential for memory and synaptic plasticity. Excessive glutamate activity via NMDA receptors can cause excitotoxic neuronal injury, which is a key mechanism of cell death in ischemic stroke and traumatic brain injury. Overactivation of glutamate receptors also contributes to the

While glutamate overactivity can worsen neurodegenerative disease, the primary neurotransmitter deficit in Parkinson disease is dopamine, not glutamate, and the hallmark symptoms arise from disrupted dopaminergic input to the striatum.

Serotonin imbalance is also implicated in migraine (treated with triptans, which are 5-HT1B/1D agonists) and in carcinoid syndrome, where excessive serotonin leads to flushing and diarrhea. Although depression and mood disorders are common comorbidities in Parkinson disease, the cardinal motor findings of bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, and postural instability stem from dopaminergic loss in the substantia nigra pars compacta, not serotonergic deficiency.

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is E. This patient presents with isolated sensory loss over the lower lateral leg, with normal motor function, reflexes, and proximal sensation, following prolonged postural compression. These findings strongly indicate an injury to the sural nerve, a purely sensory nerve that innervates the lower lateral calf and lateral foot. The sural nerve is formed from branches of the tibial and common peroneal nerves and is commonly injured through compression near the posterior calf or ankle, especially during prolonged kneeling or tight leg positions. The distribution of numbness in this case — confined to the lower lateral calf — is classic for sural neuropathy. Importantly, there are no motor deficits, ruling out involvement of the parent nerves themselves. The sural nerve is superficial along the posterolateral leg and vulnerable during meditation, yoga, or positions involving direct pressure or stretch on the calf. Its function is entirely sensory, so patients will never present with weakness or gait disturbance. The absence of sensory deficits in the medial leg (saphenous nerve) or upper lateral leg (common peroneal nerve) helps localize the lesion more precisely. A key distinction tested on USMLE is between sural and saphenous neuropathy, with lateral calf loss pointing to the sural branch and medial loss indicating saphenous involvement. The mnemonic “suraL is Lateral” helps differentiate the two. The sural nerve can also be tested on USMLE as a donor nerve for grafting due to its purely sensory function and ease of access, making it ideal for autologous nerve repair procedures. Its removal does not result in any motor deficit, which is why it's commonly harvested in clinical practice. A 29-year-old woman comes to the clinic with a complaint of numbness on the outside of her right lower leg that she noticed after returning from a weekend yoga retreat. She recalls spending an extended amount of time in kneeling and cross- legged positions during several seated meditation sessions but denies any falls or trauma. The numbness began gradually and has remained localized. She reports no pain, weakness, tingling in the foot, or balance problems. She is otherwise healthy and takes no medications. On examination, the patient has normal strength, reflexes, and range of motion throughout both lower extremities. Pinprick sensation is diminished over the lower lateral portion of the right calf, just above the lateral malleolus. Sensation is intact elsewhere, including the foot, medial leg, and upper lateral thigh. Gait is steady, and there is no tenderness or swelling of the leg. No deficits are observed proximally or contralaterally. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured?

stem: Subject System Topic
answer_text: Common peroneal (fibular) nerve

options.A: Common peroneal (fibular) nerve
options.B: Femoral nerve
options.E: Sural nerve Lower limb nerves. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: The common peroneal nerve provides motor innervation to the anterior and lateral compartments of the leg and sensory input to the upper lateral leg and dorsum of the foot. Injury causes foot drop, due to loss of dorsiflexion and eversion, along with numbness in the upper lateral shin. This patient has intact strength and gait, and her sensory loss is restricted to the lower lateral calf, well below the typical distribution of the common peroneal nerve. Additionally, there's no mention of trauma to the fibular head or foot dragging. These findings rule out a common peroneal lesion and localize the problem further downstream. Choice B: The femoral nerve innervates the anterior thigh muscles, including the quadriceps, and provides sensation to the anterior thigh and medial leg via the saphenous branch. Injury would typically present with weakness in knee extension, loss of patellar reflex, and anterior thigh numbness. This patient has no proximal weakness or reflex changes, and her numbness is lateral, not medial, which does not fit femoral nerve or its sensory branches. Furthermore, there’s no history of pelvic or groin trauma. The exam findings do not match femoral nerve territory. Choice C: The obturator nerve supplies the medial thigh muscles responsible for adduction and provides sensation to a small patch on the medial thigh. Injury causes weakness in bringing the legs together and sometimes vague medial thigh numbness, usually after pelvic surgery or trauma. This patient has no gait instability, no adduction weakness, and her sensory loss is distal and lateral, making obturator nerve injury extremely unlikely. There is also no pelvic or surgical context. The anatomic region involved is far from the obturator nerve’s pathway. Choice D: The saphenous nerve is the pure sensory branch of the femoral nerve and supplies the medial leg, including the medial calf and ankle. While it is often injured during leg surgeries or prolonged pressure over the medial knee, this patient’s numbness is on the lateral aspect of the lower calf, which is outside of the saphenous territory. Additionally, her strength and reflexes are preserved, and there's no medial sensory complaint. The distribution of loss and mechanism both argue against saphenous involvement. Bottom line: The sural nerve is a purely sensory nerve supplying the lower lateral calf and lateral foot. It is commonly injured via external compression (e.g., tight boots, kneeling, yoga), leading to isolated sensory loss without motor findings. Loss of sensation over the lower lateral leg, with normal strength and reflexes, should prompt consideration of sural neuropathy, especially when differentiating from saphenous or common peroneal injury. Although the facial nerve passes through the parotid gland, it does not supply parasympathetic fibers to it. CN VII provides parasympathetic innervation to the lacrimal, submandibular, and sublingual glands instead. Dysfunction of CN VII would not impair parotid gland secretion but would cause facial asymmetry and impaired taste anteriorly. This patient lacks any findings to suggest glossopharyngeal nerve pathology. A lesion of CN XII produces tongue deviation toward the side of the lesion due to unopposed action of the contralateral genioglossus muscle. CN XII does not carry taste fibers nor does it innervate facial expression muscles. The absence of tongue deviation and preserved speech makes hypoglossal nerve injury unlikely. This patient’s deficits are best explained by facial nerve dysfunction, not hypoglossal involvement.

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is B. This patient presents with conus medullaris syndrome (CMS), characterized by sudden-onset bilateral leg weakness, urinary retention, and perianal sensory loss. The conus medullaris, located around the L1–L2 vertebral level, contains the sacral segments that innervate the bladder, bowel, and perineum. Compression or infarction here leads to mixed upper and lower motor neuron signs, such as brisk reflexes and flaccid anal tone. In contrast to cauda equina syndrome, which develops gradually and involves peripheral nerve roots, CMS has a rapid onset with symmetric deficits. In this case, the combination of perianal anesthesia, decreased rectal tone, and bilateral motor weakness points toward CMS. The key distinction for USMLE between conus medullaris and cauda equina syndromes is that conus medullaris syndrome presents with sudden-onset symptoms and perianal anesthesia, whereas cauda equina syndrome has a gradual onset with saddle anesthesia; both can involve leg pain and urinary retention, but USMLE focuses mainly on this tempo and sensory distribution. Immediate MRI is essential for diagnosis, and prompt neurosurgical decompression is necessary to preserve neurologic function. A 65-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with sudden-onset lower back pain and weakness in both legs. She reports that the pain began abruptly this morning and has been accompanied by tingling and numbness in her inner thighs. Over the past few hours, she has developed difficulty initiating urination and describes a strange loss of sensation around her anus. She also says she feels “clumsy” when trying to walk. On examination, her lower extremities show bilateral weakness (4/5) and brisk reflexes. There is diminished sensation in the perianal region and inner thighs. Rectal tone is decreased. An urgent spinal MRI is obtained. Which of the following best explains this patient's condition?

It presents with gradual-onset symptoms, including asymmetric leg weakness, decreased reflexes, saddle anesthesia, and bladder dysfunction. Unlike conus medullaris syndrome, reflexes are

options.B: Compression of the terminal portion of the spinal cord at L1 Conus medullaris vs Cauda equina.
options.C: Demyelination of the thoracic dorsal columns Wrong answer explanations:
options.D: Ischemic infarction of the anterior spinal artery Choice A: Cauda equina syndrome results from compression of the lumbosacral nerve roots below the level of the conus medullaris, typically below L2. It presents with gradual-onset symptoms, including asymmetric leg weakness, decreased reflexes, saddle anesthesia, and bladder dysfunction. Unlike conus medullaris syndrome, reflexes are
options.E: Transverse inflammation of the lower spinal cord hyporeflexic due to the lower motor neuron nature of the nerve roots. In contrast, this patient exhibits brisk reflexes, which is an upper motor neuron sign and inconsistent with cauda equina involvement. Additionally, the symmetric sensory and motor deficits and the sudden onset of symptoms favor a lesion in the spinal cord itself rather than the nerve roots. Choice C: Demyelination of the dorsal columns would primarily affect proprioception, vibration sense, and fine touch, often sparing motor and bladder function in the early stages. This presentation is more typical of subacute combined degeneration seen in vitamin B12 deficiency. In this case, the patient has perianal anesthesia, urinary retention, and brisk reflexes, which are not characteristic of dorsal column dysfunction. Furthermore, there is no mention of dietary deficiency, anemia, or other signs that might point toward B12 deficiency or demyelinating diseases like multiple sclerosis. The acute onset also argues against a demyelinating process, which tends to progress more insidiously. Because the symptoms involve autonomic dysfunction and lower cord-level findings, dorsal column demyelination is unlikely. Choice D: Anterior spinal artery syndrome typically presents with bilateral motor paralysis and loss of pain and temperature sensation, with preservation of vibration and proprioception due to sparing of the posterior columns. It often occurs in the setting of hypotension, aortic surgery, or vascular disease, none of which are mentioned in this vignette. While urinary retention may occur, it is less prominent than in conus medullaris syndrome. The infarction usually causes a sensory level and often involves a longitudinal segment of the spinal cord, not a focal lesion at L1. Additionally, the presence of perianal sensory loss suggests a compressive lesion at the conus, not a vascular infarction. The absence of pain/temperature dissociation and vascular risk factors further makes this diagnosis unlikely. Choice E: Transverse myelitis is an inflammatory disorder affecting a segment of the spinal cord, leading to motor, sensory, and autonomic deficits, often associated with a defined sensory level. It typically evolves over hours to days, rather than the sudden onset described in this case. There is usually a preceding viral illness or known autoimmune disease, which are not reported in this patient. Additionally, MRI in transverse myelitis shows longitudinally extensive spinal cord signal changes, whereas metastatic compression would show a focal mass at the conus. Moreover, perianal anesthesia and brisk reflexes point toward conus medullaris syndrome rather than diffuse spinal cord inflammation. Bottom line: Conus medullaris syndrome results from damage to the terminal end of the spinal cord (L1–L2) and typically presents with sudden-onset bilateral motor weakness, perianal anesthesia, and urinary retention. It is distinguished from cauda equina syndrome, which is gradual in onset, involves asymmetric symptoms, and causes saddle anesthesia. Anatomy Neurology (Spinal cord disorders) None

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is D. This patient demonstrates Wernicke aphasia, characterized by fluent speech that is grammatically intact but meaningless, with impaired comprehension and lack of awareness of the deficit (anosognosia). The lesion localizes to Wernicke’s area, located in the posterior superior temporal gyrus of the dominant (usually left) hemisphere, supplied by the inferior division of the middle cerebral artery (MCA). This type of aphasia contrasts with Broca’s aphasia, which involves non-fluent but meaningful speech with preserved comprehension. Since there are no motor or sensory deficits, the infarct likely involves the temporal branch of the left MCA, sparing the primary motor and sensory cortices. High-yield associations include paraphasic errors, poor repetition, and word salad-like speech. Because patients with Wernicke aphasia are often unaware of their deficit, they may appear confused or agitated when misunderstood. Types of Aphasia: 1) Broca Aphasia: Non-fluent, impaired repetition, but intact comprehension. Patient understands but struggles to speak. When asked, “What’s your name?” they reply, “Uh… my… na… name… J… John…”—effortful, broken speech. Frustrated, since they know what they want to say. 2) Wernicke Aphasia: Fluent speech, impaired repetition, and poor comprehension. Patient speaks fluently but makes no sense. You ask, “What’s your name?” and they respond, “Yeah the sky is blue… car… alphabet soup…”—nonsensical yet fluent. Not frustrated, unaware of deficit. 3) Conduction Aphasia: Only repetition is impaired. Speech is fluent, comprehension intact. Asked to repeat “apple,” patient says “ap… ape… applesauce…”—can’t repeat despite otherwise normal language. 4) Transcortical Motor Aphasia: Like Broca but repetition is preserved. Patient struggles to generate speech but can repeat accurately. You say “cat,” they say “C… cat.” Key clue: Broca-like output, but repetition intact. A 67-year-old right-handed man is brought to the emergency department 5) Transcortical Sensory Aphasia: Like Wernicke but repetition is preserved. Patient produces nonsensical fluent speech but repeats perfectly. You say “banana,” they repeat “Banana!”—still no comprehension. after being found disoriented and speaking nonsensically. According to his 6) Mixed Transcortical Aphasia: Like global aphasia, but repetition is intact. Very limited speech and poor comprehension, but patient can still repeat words like “dog” when prompted. wife, he was watching TV when he began talking "in gibberish" and seemed 7) Global Aphasia: Non-fluent, poor comprehension, and impaired repetition. Patient can barely speak or understand and gives minimal to no meaningful response—e.g., “…uh… huh…” when asked their name. Severe across all domains. confused. He was unable to follow simple commands, and he became increasingly frustrated when people did not understand him. On exam, he speaks in long, fluent sentences with intact grammar and prosody, but his words lack meaning and are often nonsensical. He appears unaware of his language deficit. Motor and sensory strength are intact in all extremities.The diagram belows show the location of the lesion. Which of the following brain regions is most likely affected?

She reports that the symptoms began after she started taking long evening walks in new running shoes that feel tighter than her previous pair. She describes a burning pain along the outer aspect of her right lower leg and top of her

options.A: Left angular gyrus
options.B: Left caudate nucleus
options.E: Right frontal lobe
options.F: Right parietal lobe
options.G: Right temporal pole Wrong answer explanations:
options.H: Supplementary motor area Choice A: Lesions here cause Gerstmann syndrome—agraphia, acalculia, finger agnosia, and left-right disorientation—not fluent aphasia. It's in the inferior parietal lobe and involved in higher-order processing, not core language fluency. Choice B: Damage here may cause subcortical aphasia with variable speech deficits, but it's more commonly associated with frontal lobe involvement and behavioral symptoms, not fluent, nonsensical speech. Choice C: This is Broca’s area, involved in language production. Lesions here cause non-fluent aphasia with intact comprehension and insight, the opposite of this presentation. Choice E: The non-dominant frontal lobe may affect affective prosody (emotional tone of speech), not language comprehension or word content. Damage would not produce aphasia in a right-handed person. Choice F: Lesions here cause hemispatial neglect, not language deficits, especially in right-handed individuals. This area is involved in spatial awareness, not linguistic processing. Choice G: This is part of the limbic system and semantic memory network, not primary language. Damage might cause personality or memory changes but not fluent aphasia. Choice H: Involved in motor planning, and bilateral lesions may cause mutism, but not fluent aphasia with impaired comprehension. It does not localize to the temporal lobe. Bottom line: Wernicke aphasia is caused by a lesion in the posterior superior temporal gyrus of the dominant hemisphere, usually from an MCA inferior division stroke. It presents with fluent, nonsensical speech, impaired comprehension, and lack of awareness of the deficit. Anatomy Neurology (Strokes & Stroke-related stuff) None

Which of the following best explains the impaired action Choice B: Axon degeneration means the nerve fiber itself is physically breaking down, often after severe or long-lasting injury. This leads to permanent loss of signal because the “wire” is destroyed, not just its insulation. In early GBS, the axon is still structurally intact, and the problem is that myelin loss is preventing normal signal transmission. This is potentials in the affected nerves? why patients can recover well if the myelin is repaired before the axon is damaged. Axon degeneration can happen in advanced disease but is not the main cause of early weakness in demyelinating conditions. Choice C: Voltage-gated sodium channels are essential for starting and passing along action potentials in nerves. If these channels were destroyed, nerves would be unable to fire at all. In demyelinating diseases like GBS, the sodium channels are still present, but the loss of myelin makes it harder for the electrical signal to reach and activate them at the next node of Ranvier. This results in slow or blocked conduction, but the channels themselves are not the problem. So degeneration of sodium channels is not the main explanation for early conduction problems in GBS. Choice D: Microtubules form the internal “skeleton” of the axon and serve as tracks for moving materials along the nerve fiber. If they were broken down, the axon couldn’t transport needed nutrients and proteins, leading to gradual nerve dysfunction. However, in GBS, the axonal microtubules remain intact, and the weakness is due to the loss of myelin insulation. This means microtubule damage is not the reason for slowed conduction in early demyelination. While microtubule problems can affect nerve health, they are not the key factor here.

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is D. This patient has thalamic pain syndrome (Dejerine-Roussy syndrome), which occurs as a delayed complication of a prior thalamic stroke. The infarct damages the left VPL/VPM nuclei, which receive sensory input from the right side of the body and face (contralateral). Initially, patients present with pure sensory loss. Weeks to months later, some develop burning, stabbing pain, dysesthesia, and allodynia affecting the contralateral face, arm, and leg. This is due to abnormal central processing of sensory input after thalamic injury. The most common cause is a small lacunar infarct from chronic hypertension or diabetes. A 68-year-old woman presents with burning, stabbing pain on the right side of her body that has been progressively worsening over several weeks. She reports hypersensitivity to light touch on her right arm, leg, and face. Medical history includes a lacunar stroke 3 months ago. Neurologic examination shows no weakness or sensory loss to pinprick or vibration, but she withdraws to light touch. MRI shows an old infarct in the left thalamus. Which of the following best explains her current symptoms?

stem: Subject System Topic
answer_text: Degeneration of the substantia nigra

options.A: Cortical demyelination
options.B: Degeneration of the substantia nigra
options.C: Loss of spinothalamic fibers at the spinal cord level
options.E: Subthalamic nucleus infarction Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: (Cortical demyelination) Causes sensory or motor deficits seen in MS but does not explain delayed unilateral burning pain after a thalamic stroke. Choice B: (Substantia nigra degeneration) Leads to Parkinson disease (tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia), not central pain syndrome. Choice C: (Spinothalamic tract lesion) Would cause contralateral pain and temperature loss below the level of the lesion but not delayed central pain after stroke. Choice E: (Subthalamic nucleus infarct) Causes hemiballismus — flinging, involuntary movements — not chronic pain. Bottom line: Thalamic pain syndrome = delayed post-stroke central pain → lesion in VPL/VPM → contralateral burning, stabbing pain in face and body → lacunar infarct. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is B. This patient has an isolated lower motor neuron lesion of the facial nerve, resulting in complete ipsilateral facial weakness, including the forehead. The most common cause is idiopathic post-viral inflammation causing nerve edema and compression within the narrow facial canal, referred to clinically as Bell’s palsy. The absence of ear vesicles, tick exposure, or progressive symptoms supports an idiopathic etiology rather than infectious, ischemic, or mass-related causes. A 27-year-old woman presents with sudden-onset weakness of the right side of her face. She is unable to close her right eye, raise her right eyebrow, or smile symmetrically. She denies rash, ear pain, or recent travel. She reports recovering from a cold a few days ago. Neurologic examination reveals complete paralysis of the upper and lower right face. There are no vesicular lesions, hearing changes, or other neurologic findings. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her condition? A. Demyelination of corticobulbar fibers in the internal capsule

stem: Subject System Topic
answer_text: Shoulder Dermatomal distribution. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Referred pain to the anterior thigh maps to L2–L4 dermatomes via the femoral nerve, classically seen with hip joint pathology (eg, osteoarthritis, femoral neck fracture), iliopsoas irritation, or L3 radiculopathy. Although proximal ureteral colic originates around T10–T12 and can cause flank/loin pain, it does not typically project to the true anterior thigh distribution of the femoral nerve. In ureteric stones, migration to the distal ureter shifts pain toward L1–L2 dermatomes, favoring the groin rather than the anterior thigh, making this choice incorrect in this clinical setting. Choice C: Medial thigh pain follows the obturator nerve (L2–L4), often implicated in obturator hernias, pelvic sidewall pathology, or hip osteoarthritis causing adductor spasm. This distribution is not the canonical referral pattern for ureteral colic, which tracks sympathetic afferents from T10–L2 and shifts flank → lower quadrant → groin as the stone descends. Because the obturator territory is not the typical target for distal ureteral pain, medial thigh radiation is not the best answer here. Choice D: The perineum is innervated predominantly by the pudendal nerve (S2–S4) and is a hallmark referral zone for anorectal disease, pelvic floor disorders, perineal abscess, and urogenital conditions centered in the distal urethra or external genitalia. Ureteral visceral afferents do not travel with sacral parasympathetics; instead they accompany sympathetic fibers to T10–L2, explaining why perineal radiation is not expected in ureteric stone pain. Given this distinct neuroanatomy, perineal localization is inconsistent with the scenario of migrating ureteral colic. Choice E: The shoulder is a common site of referred pain from diaphragmatic irritation via the phrenic nerve (C3–C5), seen in conditions such as subphrenic abscess, splenic rupture, or peritonitis involving the diaphragm. This dermatomal distribution is entirely unrelated to ureteral innervation, which travels via sympathetic fibers from T10–L2. Because the neuroanatomic pathways are completely different, shoulder radiation would not occur in ureteral colic, making this choice entirely incorrect for this patient’s presentation. Bottom line: Ureteral colic from nephrolithiasis produces severe, colicky flank pain that changes location as the stone migrates, reflecting the T10–L2 visceral afferent pathway. When the stone reaches the distal ureter, pain is referred to the groin via shared innervation with the genitofemoral nerve (L1–L2). Anatomy Neurology (Innervation of Other organ systems) None
