answer_text: Thoracodorsal nerve against the thoracic wall. Choice E: The thoracodorsal nerve arises from the posterior cord and innervates the latissimus dorsi muscle, a large muscle involved in arm extension, adduction, and internal rotation. This nerve can indeed be injured during axillary lymph node dissection, as it travels in the axillary region. However, thoracodorsal nerve damage results in weakness with activities like climbing or swimming, not scapular winging. The latissimus dorsi does not stabilize the scapula against the thoracic wall; that role belongs to the serratus anterior. Additionally, this patient has no difficulty with powerful upper limb movements, but instead shows a prominent medial border of the scapula, indicating a serratus anterior issue. Bottom line: The long thoracic nerve innervates the serratus anterior and stabilizes the scapula. Injury leads to scapular winging, especially when pushing against resistance. Surgical trauma, particularly during mastectomy or axillary dissection, is a common cause. Anatomy Neurology (Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles) None

stem: due to a problem with PICA (posterior inferior cerebellar artery). If they don’t list PICA, you can choose vertebral artery. Choice C and D: (Medial longitudinal fasciculus): Causes internuclear ophthalmoplegia (eye movement problems), not weakness or tongue deviation. Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) is caused by a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF), which connects the abducens nucleus (CN VI) of one side to the oculomotor nucleus (CN III) of the opposite side. The result is impaired adduction of the eye on the side of the lesion during horizontal gaze, while the abducting eye shows nystagmus. For example, a right MLF lesion means the right eye can't adduct when looking left. The most common cause in young adults is multiple sclerosis (MS) due to demyelination. In older adults, stroke (brainstem ischemia) is a common cause. Choice E: (Pyramidal tract decussation): This is also part of the corticospinal tract where fibers cross in the caudal medulla. A lesion here would affect the right-sided decussating fibers, which would cause weakness on the left side of the body (contralateral to the right-sided lesion). Since this patient has right-sided weakness and tongue deviation to the left, the lesion must be on the left side, not the right.
answer_text: B Bottom line:

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is B. This patient has unilateral lower motor neuron (LMN) facial nerve (cranial nerve VII) palsy, evidenced by weakness involving both the upper and lower face on one side, including inability to wrinkle the forehead and close the eye, flattening of the nasolabial fold, and drooping of the mouth. The LMN lesion affects the facial nerve nucleus or nerve after it exits the brainstem, disrupting innervation to the muscles of facial expression, stapedius muscle, posterior belly of the digastric muscle, and stylohyoid muscle. Damage to the stapedius muscle results in hyperacusis (increased sensitivity to sound) on the affected side, due to loss of the muscle’s ability to dampen excessive oscillation of the stapes in the oval window. This is a well-recognized associated finding in Bell’s palsy, an idiopathic LMN facial nerve palsy. The facial nerve has both motor and sensory components. Motor fibers control muscles of facial expression and stapedius function, while parasympathetic fibers provide innervation to the lacrimal, submandibular, and sublingual glands. Special sensory fibers convey taste from the anterior two-thirds of the tongue via the chorda tympani branch, and general sensory fibers supply part of the external ear. LMN lesions (as in Bell’s palsy) present with complete ipsilateral facial weakness, including both upper and lower face. In contrast, upper motor neuron (UMN) lesions (eg, due to stroke involving the corticobulbar tract) spare the forehead because the upper facial muscles receive bilateral cortical innervation. A 42-year-old man presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of sudden-onset facial asymmetry. He reports that food frequently collects between his teeth and right cheek, and he has been unintentionally biting the inside of his right lip. He also notes difficulty keeping liquids from dribbling out of the right corner of his mouth when drinking. There is no history of head trauma, skin rash, ear pain, or recent upper respiratory infection. His past medical history is unremarkable, and he takes no medications. On examination, there is flattening of the right nasolabial fold, drooping of the right corner of the mouth, and incomplete closure of the right eye with attempted blinking. The forehead on the right also shows absent wrinkling with eyebrow elevation. Sensation to light touch and pinprick is intact over the entire face, and hearing is grossly normal. Cranial nerve examination is otherwise normal. Which of the following additional findings is most likely to be present in this patient?

stem: Subject System Topic
answer_text: Loss of taste sensation on the posterior third of the tongue

options.A: Anosmia on the affected side
options.B: Hyperacusis on the affected side
options.C: Loss of gag reflex on the affected side
options.D: Loss of taste sensation on the posterior third of the tongue
options.E: Ptosis with impaired pupillary constriction on the affected side UMNL VS LMNL of facial nerve. Bell’s palsy is idiopathic in most cases but is thought to be related to viral reactivation (especially herpes simplex virus type 1) causing inflammation and edema of the facial nerve in the facial canal. Other causes of LMN facial palsy include Lyme disease, Ramsay Hunt syndrome (varicella-zoster virus), sarcoidosis, HIV, otitis media, and neoplasms compressing the nerve. Bilateral LMN palsy is rare but can be seen in Guillain-Barré syndrome and Lyme disease. Clinical features include inability to close the eye (risking exposure keratitis), drooping of the mouth, loss of nasolabial fold, drooling, decreased tearing, hyperacusis, and loss of taste sensation from the anterior tongue. Management of Bell’s palsy includes early administration of high-dose corticosteroids to reduce inflammation and improve nerve recovery; antivirals (eg, acyclovir) may be added if viral reactivation is suspected. Eye protection with lubricating drops and taping shut during sleep is crucial to prevent corneal damage. Most patients recover within weeks to months, but incomplete recovery or synkinesis (involuntary movement during voluntary contraction) can occur. Identifying secondary causes (eg, through serologic testing for Lyme disease in endemic areas) is important to guide targeted therapy. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Anosmia localizes to cranial nerve I or the olfactory bulbs/tracts at the cribriform plate and frontal base; common etiologies include head trauma with shearing of olfactory fila, viral URIs, sinonasal disease, and frontal lobe lesions. The facial nerve (VII) mediates facial expression, stapedius function, parasympathetics to lacrimal/submandibular/sublingual glands, and taste to the anterior two-thirds of the tongue via the chorda tympani—none of which generate true loss of smell. In LMN facial palsy, patients may report altered taste or dry eye/mouth, but olfaction is intact. Anosmia would not be expected in isolated Bell palsy, making this choice inconsistent with the lesion described. Choice C: The gag reflex depends on glossopharyngeal nerve (IX) afferents from the posterior oropharynx and vagus nerve (X) efferents to the pharyngeal constrictors; lesions of IX/X (eg, jugular foramen syndrome, brainstem stroke) abolish the reflex and cause dysphagia, dysphonia, and uvular deviation. The facial nerve (VII) does not contribute to the gag reflex circuitry. In Bell palsy, bulbar signs are absent; speech and swallowing are preserved apart from oral incompetence from lip weakness. Loss of gag reflex would indicate a different cranial neuropathy or lateral medullary pathology rather than an isolated facial nerve palsy. Choice D: Taste from the posterior one-third of the tongue is carried by cranial nerve IX (glossopharyngeal) via the lingual branch and the inferior (petrosal) ganglion to the nucleus solitarius; CN IX also supplies the carotid sinus/body and motor to stylopharyngeus. Bell palsy affects taste from the anterior two-thirds through the chorda tympani (VII) and may reduce salivation from submandibular/sublingual glands, but it spares CN IX territory. Loss of posterior third taste points to glossopharyngeal dysfunction (eg, skull base lesion, post-tonsillectomy injury), not an LMN facial neuropathy. Choice E: Ptosis with impaired pupillary constriction reflects oculomotor (CN III) dysfunction involving levator palpebrae superioris and parasympathetic fibers from the Edinger–Westphal nucleus; additional signs include a “down-and-out” eye and diplopia, while sympathetic lesions (Horner) cause mild ptosis with miosis and anhidrosis. In Bell palsy, the eyelid cannot close due to orbicularis oculi weakness (VII), producing lagophthalmos and exposure keratopathy—not true ptosis. Pupillary light responses remain normal because parasympathetic pathways travel with CN III, not CN VII. These findings separate facial nerve palsy from third-nerve or sympathetic lesions. Bottom line: Bell palsy is an LMN lesion of the ipsilateral CN VII causing complete unilateral facial weakness, loss of anterior tongue taste, impaired tear and saliva production, and sometimes hyperacusis from stapedius paralysis. Hyperacusis results from loss of ossicle-dampening by the stapedius. UMN lesions spare the forehead due to dual innervation, but LMN lesions affect the whole side. Treatment is corticosteroids, eye protection, and addressing underlying causes. Anatomy Neurology (Disorders of peripheral nerves and muscles) None

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is D. The T10 spinal level corresponds to the umbilicus, making it the most likely level involved in a patient reporting sensory loss that begins at the navel. This patient’s findings—including lower extremity weakness, hyperreflexia, Babinski signs, and a defined sensory level at the umbilicus—are classic for thoracic spinal cord compression with upper motor neuron involvement. In patients with known malignancy, such as colon cancer, epidural metastasis is a common cause of spinal cord compression and most frequently affects the thoracic spine. The presence of urinary hesitancy and decreased rectal tone suggests autonomic involvement, which is common as spinal cord compression worsens. The patient’s “belt-like” sensation is another classic description of a sensory level. Early identification of the sensory dermatome involved—here, T10 for the umbilicus—is critical for rapid localization and appropriate imaging. MRI is the diagnostic modality of choice, and high-dose corticosteroids should be started immediately to reduce inflammation and prevent further neurologic damage. Depending on tumor sensitivity and stability, surgical decompression and/or radiation may be indicated. Spinal cord compression is a neurologic emergency, and prompt recognition of dermatomal sensory loss can make a major difference in outcome, particularly when paired with a history of known malignancy. A 67-year-old man with a history of colon cancer presents to the emergency department with worsening difficulty walking, progressive leg weakness, and a “tight belt” sensation around his abdomen. He reports that over the past several days, he has experienced numbness beginning around his belly button, which has now spread downward to both legs. He also notes new-onset urinary hesitancy and a feeling of incomplete bladder emptying. His bowel movements have become less frequent, and he describes a dull, aching mid-to-lower back pain that is worse when lying flat. On neurologic examination, his upper extremity function is intact. However, he exhibits bilateral lower extremity weakness, hyperreflexia, and sustained ankle clonus. Babinski signs are present bilaterally. Sensory testing reveals diminished pinprick and temperature sensation beginning at the level of the umbilicus and extending caudally. Light touch, proprioception, and vibration are preserved. Rectal tone is reduced. An urgent MRI of the spine reveals a compressive epidural mass impinging on the spinal cord at a thoracic level. Which of the following spinal cord levels is

stem: most likely affected?
answer_text: T4

options.A: L1
options.B: T4
options.E: T12 Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: L1 corresponds to the inguinal region, which is below the umbilicus. While L1 involvement may contribute to motor findings or localized groin numbness, it does not match the patient’s description of sensory loss beginning at the umbilical level. L1 is too low to explain the observed sensory level. Choice B: T4 corresponds to the nipple line, which is significantly higher than the umbilicus. A lesion at this level would result in a sensory level at the mid-chest, not the mid-abdomen. This discrepancy in dermatomal distribution rules out T4 as the correct level. Choice C: T8 corresponds to a level just below the xiphoid process, which is still above the umbilicus. A lesion at T8 would cause a sensory level higher than this patient’s symptoms suggest. Given that the numbness begins at the navel, T8 is too high to be responsible for the deficit. Choice E: T12 innervates the suprapubic region, which is below the umbilicus. T12 involvement could affect lower abdominal sensation but not that of the umbilical level. The sensory level seen in this patient clearly begins at T10, making T12 too low. Bottom line: T10 innervates the umbilical dermatome, and sensory loss starting at the umbilicus suggests thoracic spinal cord compression at T10. This is a medical emergency in patients with metastatic cancer and warrants urgent MRI and corticosteroids to prevent irreversible damage. Anatomy Neurology (Spinal cord disorders) None Subject System Topic A 55-year-old man is admitted to the ICU for altered mental status and severe hyponatremia (serum Na⁺ = 112 mEq/L). He is treated with IV hypertonic saline. Over the next 48 hours, his sodium level rises to 137 mEq/L. On day 3, he becomes acutely unresponsive. Neurologic exam reveals complete quadriplegia, but he can open his eyes and blink to command. He cannot speak, move his face, or breathe spontaneously. MRI shows symmetric hyperintensity in the central pons, sparing the tegmentum. Which of the following structures is most likely affected?

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is B. This patient has developed central pontine myelinolysis (CPM), now more broadly categorized as osmotic demyelination syndrome, following rapid correction of chronic hyponatremia. The hallmark is selective demyelination of the central pons, particularly the basis pontis, which contains descending corticospinal and corticobulbar tracts. Destruction of these motor tracts leads to locked-in syndrome: patients are awake and aware, with preserved vertical eye movements and blinking, but are completely paralyzed and unable to speak (anarthria). MRI reveals T2 hyperintensity in the central pons, sparing the tegmentum and other dorsal structures that control consciousness and eye movement, which explains why these patients remain alert and can blink. This condition mimics basilar artery stroke, but the cause here is metabolic rather than vascular. The key association for Step 1 is overcorrection of hyponatremia, especially when sodium rises >8–10 mEq/L in 24 hours.

stem: Choice D: While these tracts mediate voluntary motor control, medullary lesions usually cause contralateral weakness, not the complete paralysis seen in locked-in syndrome. Also, the MRI here localizes to the pons, not the medulla. Choice E: This carries fine touch and proprioception, not motor commands. Its involvement causes sensory deficits, not paralysis or eye findings. Choice F: Like choice D, these carry corticospinal tracts but do not explain the MRI findings localized to the pons, nor do they cause complete paralysis with preserved consciousness. Choice G: Loss of dopaminergic neurons here causes Parkinson disease, with resting tremor, rigidity, and bradykinesia—not acute paralysis or locked-in syndrome. Choice H: This area is spared in central pontine myelinolysis and houses structures necessary for consciousness and vertical eye movement, which remain intact. Its involvement would produce coma, not the locked-in state.
answer_text: Basis pontis Central pontine myelinolysis from rapid sodium correction damages the basis pontis, leading to locked-in syndrome with preserved consciousness and vertical eye movement but complete motor paralysis.

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is B. This patient has medial medullary syndrome. You know this because of three key findings: weakness on one side of the body, loss of vibration and position sense on the same side, and tongue deviation that points to the side of the lesion. In this case, his tongue deviates to the left, so the lesion is on the left medial medulla. This is usually due to occlusion of the anterior spinal artery (ASA). Just remember: tongue deviation = medial medulla; it points toward the side of the lesion. A 72-year-old man is brought to the hospital because of sudden weakness. On exam, he has weakness of his right arm and leg, decreased vibration and position sense on the right side, and his tongue deviates to the left when protruded. Which of the following areas is most likely involved?

stem: Subject System Topic
answer_text: Right medial medulla Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: lateral medulla gives cranial nerve IX/X findings (hoarseness, dysphagia), not tongue deviation. Choice C: pons lesions usually affect cranial nerve VI (abducens palsy) or VII (facial droop), not tongue deviation or limb weakness. Choice D: lateral medulla gives hoarseness, dysphagia, and Horner syndrome — not tongue deviation or full body weakness. Choice E: medial medulla would fit, but the lesion is on the left, not the right, because the tongue points to the left. Bottom line: Tongue deviation and body weakness = medial medulla on the same side as the tongue points. Think ASA. Anatomy Neurology (Strokes & Stroke-related stuff) None

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is E. The jugular foramen transmits cranial nerves IX (glossopharyngeal), X (vagus), and XI (accessory), as well as the internal jugular vein, making it a key structure for multiple vital neurologic and vascular pathways. Cranial nerve IX (glossopharyngeal) provides sensory innervation to the posterior third of the tongue and the oropharynx, including the afferent limbs of both the gag and cough reflexes, and also contributes parasympathetic fibers to the parotid gland. Cranial nerve X (vagus) supplies motor fibers to the muscles of the soft palate, pharynx, and larynx, which are essential for phonation, swallowing, and the efferent limb of the gag and cough reflexes, and it also mediates autonomic innervation to thoracic and abdominal viscera. The accessory nerve (XI) innervates the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles, supporting head rotation and shoulder elevation. Damage to the structures traversing the jugular foramen produces jugular foramen (Vernet) syndrome, a constellation of deficits including dysphagia due to pharyngeal muscle paralysis, hoarseness from vocal cord paralysis, diminished gag and cough reflexes, and possible ipsilateral shoulder weakness from trapezius and sternocleidomastoid involvement. Uvular deviation away from the lesion side reflects impaired vagus innervation to the ipsilateral palatal muscles, resulting in unopposed contralateral contraction. The combination of impaired swallowing, hoarseness, loss of gag and cough reflexes, and uvular deviation strongly localizes the lesion to the jugular foramen, distinguishing it from lesions involving other cranial nerve exit foramina. A 55-year-old man is brought to the emergency department after his wife noticed that he is unable to swallow liquids without choking and that his voice has become hoarse. On examination, he has a diminished gag reflex and no cough reflex when the posterior pharyngeal wall is stimulated. Neurologic examination reveals normal motor strength and intact sensation in the upper and lower extremities. His uvula deviates to the left upon phonation. Imaging reveals a lesion affecting the cranial nerves responsible for these findings. The sensory afferents responsible for these symptoms pass through which of the following foramina?

stem: The correct answer is H

options.A: Cribriform plate
options.B: Foramen magnum
options.E: Jugular foramen Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: The cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone transmits the olfactory nerve (CN I), responsible for smell. Damage here results in anosmia but would not affect swallowing, phonation, or pharyngeal reflexes. No other cranial nerves or vessels involved in this patient’s presentation pass through this foramina. Fracture of the cribriform plate may also lead to CSF rhinorrhea due to its communication with the anterior cranial fossa. This structure is unrelated to the gag or cough reflex. Choice B: The foramen magnum transmits the medulla oblongata, spinal roots of the accessory nerve (XI), vertebral arteries, and meninges. It allows communication between the cranial cavity and spinal canal but does not transmit cranial nerves IX or X, which are responsible for the gag and cough reflex afferents. Lesions here may cause quadriparesis and medullary dysfunction but would not selectively impair oropharyngeal sensation. The accessory nerve fibers enter through this foramen but exit through the jugular foramen. This foramen is anatomically unrelated to the presenting signs in this patient. Choice C: The foramen ovale transmits the mandibular division of the trigeminal nerve (V3), which supplies general sensation to the lower face, anterior two-thirds of the tongue, and motor innervation to the muscles of mastication. Damage here produces jaw weakness and mandibular sensory loss but not dysphagia, hoarseness, or loss of gag reflexes. The lesser petrosal nerve may also pass through but is unrelated to pharyngeal or laryngeal function. The glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves do not traverse this foramen. This option does not explain this patient's constellation of findings. Choice D: The foramen rotundum transmits the maxillary division of the trigeminal nerve (V2), which provides sensory innervation to the midface, including the cheek and upper lip. Lesions here would result in sensory loss over the V2 dermatome but would spare swallowing, phonation, and pharyngeal reflexes. The glossopharyngeal and vagus nerves do not traverse this foramen. Damage to V2 would not produce uvular deviation or hoarseness. This patient’s clinical findings are unrelated to pathology at the foramen rotundum. Bottom line: The jugular foramen transmits cranial nerves IX, X, and XI and is essential for pharyngeal sensation, phonation, and swallowing. Lesions at the jugular foramen cause jugular foramen (Vernet) syndrome, characterized by diminished gag reflex, hoarseness, dysphagia, and uvular deviation. Anatomy Neurology (Nerve injuries)

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is H. The tibial nerve, a branch of the sciatic nerve, runs through the popliteal fossa and down the posterior leg, innervating the posterior compartment muscles responsible for plantarflexion, inversion, and toe flexion. It also provides sensory innervation to the sole of the foot. This patient’s inability to plantarflex (can’t stand on tippytoes) or invert the foot, along with sensory loss over the sole, is characteristic of tibial nerve injury, especially following trauma to the popliteal fossa. Because dorsiflexion, toe extension, and eversion are preserved (functions of the peroneal nerve branches), the lesion is localized to the tibial nerve rather than a more proximal sciatic lesion. Reflexes are intact, suggesting the lesion is distal to the spinal nerve roots but proximal to the ankle (as in tarsal tunnel syndrome). A 33-year-old man presents to the emergency department with right foot weakness and pain after sustaining a deep laceration to the popliteal fossa during a construction site accident. He is unable to stand on his toes or push off while walking. On examination, there is weakness in plantarflexion and inversion of the right foot, and decreased flexion of the toes. Sensation is diminished over the sole of the foot. Dorsiflexion, toe extension, and eversion are intact. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are normal. Which of the following nerves is most likely injured? A. Common peroneal nerve B. Deep peroneal nerve C.

stem: Choice B: The axillary nerve innervates the deltoid muscle and provides sensation to the lateral upper arm. It is commonly injured in shoulder trauma and presents with impaired abduction of the arm (15–90°) and flattening of the shoulder contour. This patient has no shoulder weakness or sensory loss in the upper arm. The symptoms are distal and localized to the hand, making axillary nerve involvement unlikely. Label B correctly identifies the axillary nerve, but it does not correspond to the clinical findings. Choice C: The radial nerve supplies the extensors of the wrist and fingers and provides sensation to the dorsal hand and posterior arm. Injury often results in wrist drop, weak grip, and dorsal hand numbness, especially following humeral fractures. This patient has no weakness in wrist or finger extension and no dorsal hand sensory complaints. The motor and sensory pattern is inconsistent with radial nerve dysfunction, and label C, although anatomically correct, is not implicated here.

explanation: The correct answer is E. This patient presents with loss of plantarflexion and inversion of the foot following an ankle injury, while dorsiflexion and eversion remain intact. This pattern of deficits strongly points toward injury to the tibial nerve, which innervates the posterior compartment of the leg, including the gastrocnemius, soleus, tibialis posterior, and flexor digitorum longus muscles. These muscles are responsible for plantarflexion (pushing off the foot) and inversion, both of which are impaired here. The tibial nerve is a branch of the sciatic nerve and courses posteriorly through the leg, making it vulnerable to injury at the posterior ankle or tarsal tunnel. The inability to stand on the toes of the affected foot is a classic sign of tibial nerve dysfunction. Since dorsiflexion (deep peroneal) and eversion (superficial peroneal) are intact, the injury must A 34-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic due to difficulty walking after slipping and falling spare the anterior and lateral compartments of the leg, localizing the lesion to the tibial distribution. options.D: Superficial peroneal (fibular) nerve Choice D: The superficial peroneal nerve innervates the lateral compartment of the leg, which controls foot eversion, and provides sensory innervation to the dorsum of the foot and lateral lower leg. The deficits do not fit the superficial peroneal distribution, and the mechanism of injury also favors a deeper structure like the tibial nerve. Tibial nerve Bottom line: The tibial nerve innervates the posterior compartment of the leg and is responsible for plantarflexion and inversion. Injury results in inability to stand on tiptoes and weakness pushing off the ground, with preserved dorsiflexion and eversion. This patient’s history of recent respiratory illness, progressive weakness, and reduced deep tendon reflexes supports the diagnosis. GBS frequently begins with distal weakness. He reports mild shortness of breath and says his hands now feel paresthesias followed by ascending, symmetric weakness and hyporeflexia or areflexia. stem: often increased in MS, unlike the reduced reflexes seen in GBS. The diagnosis is based on MRI findings of CNS lesions disseminated in time and space. Choice D: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder targeting the acetylcholine receptors at the neuromuscular junction, leading to fatigable muscle weakness. It typically presents with ptosis, diplopia, and bulbar symptoms, and progresses in a descending pattern. Reflexes and sensation are typically preserved, and there is no autonomic dysfunction. MG affects the neuromuscular junction, a key distinction from GBS, which affects the peripheral nervous system. Treatments include acetylcholinesterase inhibitors, corticosteroids, and immunomodulators. Choice E: Transverse myelitis is a focal inflammation of the spinal cord, leading to motor, sensory, and autonomic dysfunction below the lesion level. While it can present acutely with limb weakness, it is typically localized, and findings are often asymmetric. MRI shows a focal spinal cord lesion, and CSF usually shows pleocytosis, not the albuminocytologic dissociation characteristic of GBS. Transverse myelitis may mimic GBS but lacks the symmetric ascending weakness and peripheral nerve findings.
answer_text: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis

options.A: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis
options.B: Guillain-Barré syndrome Bottom line:
options.C: Multiple sclerosis Guillain-Barré syndrome presents with symmetric ascending paralysis, areflexia, and autonomic instability following a recent infection. It affects the peripheral nervous system, in contrast to MG (neuromuscular junction) and MS (CNS). Early recognition and treatment with IVIg or plasmapheresis are critical to prevent respiratory failure and other complications.

Clinical manifestations typically appear in early childhood, with delayed walking, frequent falls, Gowers sign, and pseudohypertrophy of the calves. As the disease ophthalmoplegia or fatigability with repeated muscle use. MRI of the brain is progresses, patients lose ambulation by adolescence and develop cardiomyopathy and respiratory failure. In contrast to ALS, DMD does not present in adulthood, does not involve upper motor neuron signs, and does not feature fasciculations. unremarkable. Routine laboratory testing, including vitamin B12 levels, is within Furthermore, sensory function remains intact in DMD, but UMN findings like Babinski reflexes are absent, helping to distinguish it from ALS. The late-onset, asymmetric weakness and UMN/LMN combination in this patient’s case is incompatible with the presentation of DMD. normal limits. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Choice D: Lambert-Eaton syndrome (LES) is a disorder of presynaptic neuromuscular transmission most often associated with paraneoplastic syndromes, particularly small cell lung carcinoma. It is caused by autoantibodies against voltage-gated calcium channels, impairing acetylcholine release at the neuromuscular junction. Patients typically present with symmetric proximal muscle weakness that improves with repeated activity, in contrast to the fatigable weakness of myasthenia gravis. Hyporeflexia is common, and autonomic symptoms such as dry mouth, constipation, and erectile dysfunction frequently occur. Bulbar and ocular involvement is usually mild, and UMN signs like hyperreflexia and Babinski are not part of the disease profile. This patient’s brisk reflexes, lack of autonomic features, and combined UMN/LMN findings strongly argue against LES. Additionally, fasciculations and asymmetric atrophy are not characteristic of LES, further excluding it. Choice E: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is an autoimmune demyelinating disease of the central nervous system characterized by recurrent neurologic deficits separated in time and space. Common presentations include optic neuritis, internuclear ophthalmoplegia, sensory disturbances, limb weakness, and bladder/bowel dysfunction. Reflexes are often brisk due to corticospinal tract involvement, but LMN signs such as fasciculations and muscle atrophy are not seen because MS does not cause degeneration of anterior horn cells. Sensory deficits are a hallmark of MS, including impaired vibration sense, proprioception, or pain/temperature perception, which are entirely absent in this patient. The steady, relentless progression without distinct relapses or remissions is more typical of ALS than MS. Finally, MRI findings in MS reveal demyelinating plaques in the brain or spinal cord, whereas ALS imaging is often normal or shows only nonspecific changes. The absence of sensory involvement and presence of LMN signs make MS very unlikely in this case.

stem: Choices A, B and C: Detrusor underactivity, outlet obstruction, and overflow incontinence all cause impaired bladder emptying, but their mechanisms and presentations differ from this patient’s case. Detrusor underactivity occurs when the bladder muscle cannot generate enough contractile force during micturition, often due to diabetic autonomic neuropathy, spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis, or chronic overdistension. Outlet obstruction, in contrast, results from narrowing of the bladder neck or urethra—seen in conditions such as urethral stricture, pelvic tumor, bladder neck contracture, or benign prostatic hyperplasia in men—which raises detrusor pressures during voiding and causes hesitancy, weak stream, and postvoid dribbling. Both detrusor underactivity and outlet obstruction can lead to overflow incontinence, in which bladder capacity is exceeded and urine dribbles constantly; this is accompanied by a distended bladder, high postvoid residual volumes, and often recurrent urinary tract infections. In this patient, postvoid residual volume is normal, bladder emptying is not impaired, and symptoms are triggered by urgency rather than continuous leakage. There is no history of hesitancy, weak stream, straining to void, or incomplete emptying, and the course is not suggestive of chronic bladder dysfunction. These features make detrusor underactivity, outlet obstruction, and overflow incontinence unlikely explanations for her presentation.

She reports immediate pain and difficulty lifting the arm. She has a history of osteoporosis and lives independently. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the proximal humerus. Active

subject: General
explanation: range of motion is limited due to pain, but she can move her fingers and wrist The correct answer is B. normally. Radial and ulnar pulses are intact. An x-ray confirms the diagnosis. This patient's presentation is classic for axillary nerve injury, the most common neurologic complication of a surgical neck fracture of the humerus, as confirmed on plain x-ray. The axillary nerve courses around the surgical neck, passing through the quadrangular space, making it especially vulnerable to traction or compression injuries when the proximal humerus is fractured. Although this fracture is described as minimally displaced, even nondisplaced or low-energy fractures at this location can injure the nerve due to its close anatomical proximity. The hallmark findings of axillary nerve injury include sensory loss over the lateral shoulder (the “regimental badge” area) and potential motor weakness of the deltoid, which may manifest as impaired shoulder abduction once the acute pain subsides. In this case, the x-ray confirms the location of the fracture at the surgical neck, and the patient’s reported numbness over the lateral shoulder is highly specific for axillary nerve involvement. Motor testing may be limited by pain in the acute setting, but the sensory findings are crucial and should always be documented. Most axillary nerve injuries in the context of closed surgical neck fractures are neuropraxias, meaning they often recover spontaneously over weeks to months. However, persistent sensory deficits or weakness warrant follow-up with repeat neurologic exams and possibly electrodiagnostic testing. Recognizing the association between surgical neck fractures and axillary nerve injury—especially when x-ray confirms the fracture—is essential for appropriate diagnosis, patient counseling, and monitoring.

This nerve is typically injured during posterior triangle neck dissections or trauma to the lateral neck—not shoulder falls or humeral fractures. The spinal accessory nerve does not provide Which of the following is the most likely associated neurologic finding in this patient? cutaneous sensation to the shoulder and is anatomically too distant from the surgical neck to be involved. Choice D: Scapular winging during forward flexion is caused by injury to the long thoracic nerve, which innervates the serratus anterior. This condition is classically seen in repetitive overhead activity, trauma, or surgical injury (e.g., during lymph node dissection). The nerve courses along the chest wall and is not affected by fractures of the humerus. Scapular winging is a motor deficit and would not present with localized shoulder numbness. Choice E: Weakness of internal rotation due to subscapular nerve injury would occur if there were damage to the upper or lower subscapular nerves, which innervate the subscapularis and teres major muscles. These nerves originate from the posterior cord of the brachial plexus and are deep within the axilla. They are unlikely to be injured in isolation from a humeral neck fracture. Additionally, this patient’s primary complaint is sensory, not internal rotation weakness.

stem: Choice C: Saphenous nerve is a pure sensory branch of the femoral nerve that supplies the medial leg and ankle. It does not innervate any muscles and has no role in motor function. Injury to the saphenous nerve might cause medial leg numbness but cannot explain foot drop or toe extension weakness. The patient's motor deficits and localized sensory loss make this an incorrect choice. Saphenous nerve injury might occur during knee surgery or trauma to the medial leg, which is not seen here. Choice D: Superficial peroneal nerve innervates the lateral compartment muscles (fibularis longus and brevis), which evert the foot, and supplies sensation to most of the dorsum of the foot, excluding the first web space. This patient has intact eversion and normal sensation over most of the dorsum, except between the first and second toes, indicating that the superficial branch is functioning normally. Therefore, this is not a superficial peroneal nerve injury, as the distribution of sensory and motor loss is inconsistent.

Furthermore, the location of trauma (anterior shin) does not anatomically correspond to the tibial nerve's pathway.

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is D. This patient has internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO), a classic result of a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF), which coordinates horizontal eye movements by linking the abducens nucleus (CN VI) of one side to the oculomotor nucleus (CN III) of the other. In a leftward gaze, the right MLF carries the signal from the left abducens nucleus to the right oculomotor nucleus. If the right MLF is damaged, the right eye cannot adduct, while the left eye abducts with nystagmus due to overcorrection. Convergence remains intact because it uses a separate pathway. INO is highly associated with multiple sclerosis in younger adults, often bilateral. In contrast, in older patients, unilateral INO is more often caused by brainstem stroke. This patient’s MRI findings (periventricular plaques) and prior transient neurologic symptoms further support demyelination in MS. Pupillary reflex and convergence sparing help localize the lesion specifically to the MLF, rather than CN III or its nucleus. A 28-year-old woman presents to the clinic with double vision, especially when looking to the left. She also reports fatigue and numbness in her lower limbs that resolved spontaneously a few weeks ago. On exam, when asked to look to the left, her right eye fails to adduct past the midline, while her left eye abducts with a horizontal jerk. Pupillary light reflexes and convergence are intact. MRI of the brain shows periventricular white matter hyperintensities. Which of the following structures is most likely affected in this patient?

options.H: Superior colliculus Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: A lesion here would cause ipsilateral lateral rectus palsy, preventing abduction of the eye on the same side. It would not cause isolated impaired adduction of the contralateral eye. Choice B: Damage would cause ataxia and coordination deficits, not isolated eye movement problems. The cerebellar peduncles do not mediate horizontal gaze. Choice C: This mediates pupillary constriction and is involved in light and accommodation reflexes. It is not involved in horizontal eye movement coordination. Choice E: A lesion here would impair adduction, elevation, and depression of the affected eye along with ptosis and pupil dilation—not isolated adduction failure with preserved convergence. Choice F: This coordinates horizontal gaze by stimulating the ipsilateral abducens nucleus. A lesion here would prevent conjugate gaze toward the side of the lesion, not isolated internuclear ophthalmoplegia. Choice G: Compression can cause Parinaud syndrome (upward gaze palsy, light-near dissociation), not INO. It has no role in horizontal gaze. Choice H: Involved in reflex gaze shifts and vertical eye movements, but not in coordination of horizontal gaze between CN VI and CN III. Bottom line: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia is due to a lesion in the medial longitudinal fasciculus, most often caused by multiple sclerosis in young patients, resulting in impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye and nystagmus of the abducting eye. Choice E: Pituitary adenoma is rare in children and typically presents in adults with hormonal hypersecretion (eg, prolactinoma, GH-secreting adenoma) or mass effect on the optic chiasm causing bitemporal hemianopsia. Pediatric cases more often involve functioning adenomas producing precocious puberty or gigantism rather than hormone deficiency. Calcifications are not a typical imaging finding in pituitary adenomas. Furthermore, adenomas usually grow slowly and may not produce severe symptoms in a short timeframe, whereas this patient’s progression over months with significant endocrine dysfunction is more consistent with a craniopharyngioma. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is E. Optic neuritis is a classic presentation in multiple sclerosis (MS), a demyelinating disease of the central nervous system affecting predominantly young women. In MS, autoimmune-mediated destruction of oligodendrocytes leads to demyelination in the optic nerve (cranial nerve II), resulting in acute, painful monocular vision loss, reduced visual acuity, and a relative afferent pupillary defect (Marcus Gunn pupil). This is observed when shining light in the affected eye causes both pupils to constrict less than when light is shone in the unaffected eye, due to diminished afferent input to the Edinger-Westphal nucleus. Multiple sclerosis is a chronic autoimmune CNS disease mainly affecting young women, associated with HLA-DRB1 and factors like low vitamin D and EBV. Diagnosis involves MRI lesions and CSF oligoclonal bands. Treatment includes steroids for relapses, disease-modifying agents (eg, ocrelizumab), baclofen for spasticity, and bethanechol for urinary retention. Prognosis varies. Urge incontinence in MS results from demyelination of spinal tracts controlling bladder function, and upper motor neuron signs (such as clonus) arise from involvement of the corticospinal tracts. MRI findings of periventricular plaques and the clinical triad of optic neuritis, incontinence, and UMN signs are highly suggestive of MS. The illustration referenced depicts the pathway of light through the affected and unaffected eyes and the subsequent pupillary response in a patient with Marcus Gunn pupil: A 29-year-old woman presents with intermittent visual blurring in her right eye and involuntary muscle spasms of her left hand that started 10 days ago. She describes a sudden, sharp pain behind her right eye followed by difficulty focusing, especially in low light. She also notes frequent, urgent trips to the restroom, totaling over 10 urinations daily, with occasional loss of urinary control. Neurologic examination demonstrates hyperreflexia and clonus in the left upper extremity, along with decreased sensation to vibration in the right lower limb. Cranial nerve assessment reveals a subtle afferent pupillary defect on the right. Family history is negative for neurodegenerative disorders, but her mother has systemic lupus erythematosus. Funduscopic exam is notable for mild optic disc swelling. MRI of the brain shows multiple hyperintense lesions in the periventricular white matter. Which of the following clinical findings is most likely in this patient?

stem: Subject System Topic A 12-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department after falling off the monkey bars at school. He landed on his outstretched right hand and immediately felt pain at his elbow. He has no significant past medical history. On examination, there is swelling and tenderness over the medial aspect of the right elbow. He keeps the arm flexed and resists attempts at extension due to pain. There is decreased
answer_text: Optic neuritis Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO) presents as sudden, painless, complete monocular vision loss, often associated with a cherry-red spot on fundus exam and a history of vascular risk factors (e.g., atrial fibrillation, carotid atherosclerosis). It does not cause eye pain, afferent pupillary defect, or signs of demyelination elsewhere in the CNS, such as clonus or urinary incontinence. CRAO is an ophthalmologic emergency but is not associated with demyelinating plaques or young women. Choice B: Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) is an acute, immune-mediated demyelinating polyneuropathy affecting the peripheral nervous system, with ascending weakness, areflexia, and possible autonomic instability. GBS does not involve central demyelination, optic neuritis, or upper motor neuron findings. It typically follows a gastrointestinal or respiratory infection (e.g., Campylobacter jejuni) and is managed with IVIG or plasmapheresis. GBS spares the optic nerve, which is part of the CNS. Choice C: Internuclear ophthalmoplegia (INO) results from demyelination of the medial longitudinal fasciculus (MLF), also seen in MS. INO is characterized by impaired adduction of the ipsilateral eye and nystagmus of the contralateral eye during attempted lateral gaze. Unlike optic neuritis, INO does not present with acute vision loss or pain; instead, patients complain of diplopia. Clinical examination shows preserved pupillary light reflex, distinguishing it from optic neuritis. INO is highly associated with MS but is not the best match for the patient's acute monocular visual loss and afferent pupillary defect. Choice D: Myasthenia gravis is an autoimmune disorder targeting the acetylcholine receptor at the neuromuscular junction, causing fluctuating skeletal muscle weakness and fatigability, especially of ocular and bulbar muscles. It causes ptosis and diplopia but does not present with optic nerve dysfunction, afferent pupillary defect, upper motor neuron signs, or MRI findings of white matter plaques. Treatment includes acetylcholinesterase inhibitors and immunosuppression, not disease-modifying therapy for demyelination. Bottom line: Optic neuritis is a highly testable initial manifestation of multiple sclerosis, presenting with painful, acute, monocular vision loss and a relative afferent pupillary defect. MS should be considered in young women with CNS demyelination symptoms, including upper motor neuron findings and urinary incontinence. Pathology Neurology (Demyelinating diseases) None

Radial pulse is The ulnar nerve is most commonly injured in medial epicondylar fractures, especially in pediatric patients. This nerve travels posterior to the medial epicondyle in the cubital tunnel, making it highly vulnerable to traction or entrapment during avulsion or displacement of the medial epicondyle. The classic signs of ulnar nerve injury include sensory loss present, and capillary refill in the hand is normal. An x-ray confirms the diagnosis. over the medial one and a half digits (fourth and fifth fingers) and weakness of intrinsic hand muscles, often resulting in impaired grip and difficulty extending or abducting the fingers. Children may present with clawing of the hand, particularly when trying to extend the fourth and fifth digits. In this case, the child exhibits both medial elbow tenderness (corresponding to the fracture site) and ulnar nerve–specific deficits, including sensory loss and impaired extension of the ulnar-innervated fingers. These findings strongly implicate the ulnar nerve as the affected structure. Even if radiographic displacement appears minimal, the proximity of the ulnar nerve to the medial epicondyle means that nerve traction or contusion can still occur. Early identification of ulnar nerve dysfunction is essential, as persistent compression or entrapment may require surgical intervention. In most cases, however, conservative treatment with splinting and monitoring is sufficient, especially if nerve function shows signs of recovery. Persistent or worsening symptoms may prompt advanced imaging or referral to orthopedics for possible exploration or decompression.

stem: Subject System Topic A 5-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric neurology clinic for evaluation of gait abnormalities and poor coordination. Her parents report that she began walking later than other children and has always had trouble maintaining her balance. Over the past year, her teachers have noticed she struggles with tasks that require fine motor skills, such as using scissors or holding a pencil. The parents also note that she has
answer_text: Prior episode of rheumatic fever

options.A: Excessive alcohol consumption
options.B: History of spinal trauma
options.E: Recent vegetarian dietary changes Syringomyelia - Cape-like lesion. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Chronic alcohol use is associated with several neurological complications, including cerebellar degeneration (leading to gait ataxia), peripheral neuropathy (with symmetric stocking-glove sensory loss), and Wernicke-Korsakoff syndrome due to thiamine deficiency. However, these conditions do not produce the classic dissociated sensory loss seen in this patient, where pain and temperature sensation are selectively impaired with preserved proprioception. Additionally, alcohol-related neuropathies typically begin distally in the lower limbs and progress proximally, rather than presenting with segmental upper limb weakness and sensory loss in a cape-like distribution. Choice C: Renal cell carcinoma is well known for paraneoplastic syndromes such as polycythemia due to erythropoietin secretion or hypercalcemia from PTHrP. It is also associated with metastases to bone, lung, and brain. However, it is not a recognized cause of syrinx formation or central cord pathology. Although renal cell carcinoma may rarely invade the spinal column or epidural space, such lesions typically produce cord compression, resulting in upper motor neuron signs like hyperreflexia and spasticity, rather than the flaccid weakness with selective sensory deficits seen in this patient. Choice D: Rheumatic fever is a post-streptococcal autoimmune condition that primarily affects the heart, joints, and central nervous system, with Sydenham chorea being its most notable neurologic manifestation. Sydenham chorea presents as involuntary, purposeless movements of the face and limbs, often in children and adolescents. It does not cause segmental motor atrophy, sensory dissociation, or spinal cord pathology. Rheumatic carditis may cause long-term valvular disease, but it is unrelated to the development of syringomyelia, which arises from structural or congenital abnormalities in CSF flow dynamics. Choice E: A vegetarian diet, if not well-balanced, may lead to vitamin B12 deficiency, which causes subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord. This condition typically affects the dorsal columns and lateral corticospinal tracts, leading to loss of vibration and proprioception, spastic weakness, and positive Babinski signs. It may also present with peripheral neuropathy. However, B12 deficiency does not spare proprioception while selectively impairing pain and temperature sensation. The pattern of cape-like dissociated sensory loss and lower motor neuron findings in the upper limbs seen here are not features of B12 deficiency. Bottom line: Syringomyelia is a central spinal cord disorder characterized by loss of pain and temperature sensation in a cape-like distribution with preserved proprioception, often accompanied by hand weakness and muscle atrophy. The most common acquired risk factor in adults is prior spinal trauma, which can lead to disruption of CSF flow and syrinx formation years later. Pathophysiology Neurology (Spinal cord disorders) None

Dandy–Walker malformation is a congenital brain malformation characterized by absence of the cerebellar vermis and cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle. These structural abnormalities disrupt normal cerebellar function, leading to truncal ataxia, delayed motor development, and impaired coordination. This patient’s delayed walking, broad-based Neurologic testing reveals difficulty with rapid alternating movements and finger-to- gait, truncal instability, and difficulty with coordination tasks are consistent with cerebellar dysfunction from such a malformation. nose testing. Muscle tone and strength are normal in all extremities. An MRI of the MRI findings typically reveal an enlarged posterior fossa with a cystic structure representing the dilated fourth ventricle and hypoplasia or agenesis of the cerebellar vermis. The image provided demonstrates these features. Unlike acute cerebellar lesions, symptoms develop early in life and progress slowly as the child grows. In some cases, brain is performed and is shown below. hydrocephalus may also be present due to obstruction of cerebrospinal fluid pathways. The clinical presentation may include motor delay, balance issues, and sometimes cognitive impairment. This patient has no signs of acute intracranial pressure, but her developmental trajectory and exam findings fit the diagnosis. The diagnosis is made based on neuroimaging findings and clinical presentation. Management is supportive, focusing on physical and occupational therapy to improve coordination and motor function, with surgical intervention reserved for complications like hydrocephalus.

stem: Choice B: Ataxia-telangiectasia is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder with cerebellar atrophy, oculocutaneous telangiectasias, and immunodeficiency. Onset is usually in early childhood with progressive ataxia, but MRI shows cerebellar shrinkage rather than cystic enlargement of the fourth ventricle. The absence of telangiectasias, recurrent infections, and immune issues here argues against this diagnosis. Choice C: Medulloblastoma is a malignant cerebellar tumor typically presenting in children with signs of increased intracranial pressure, such as headache, vomiting, and papilledema, due to obstruction of CSF flow. This patient has no signs of acute intracranial hypertension, and the MRI does not show a solid mass lesion — instead, it demonstrates structural malformation. Medulloblastomas are also not associated with absent cerebellar vermis. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis? Choice E: Multiple sclerosis is a demyelinating disease with episodes of neurologic deficits separated in time and space, often presenting in young adults, not early childhood. MRI findings include periventricular white matter lesions, not congenital cerebellar malformations. This patient’s slowly evolving developmental symptoms and congenital MRI abnormalities exclude MS. Bottom line: Dandy–Walker malformation = congenital absence of cerebellar vermis + cystic dilation of the fourth ventricle. Presents in childhood with truncal ataxia, delayed motor milestones, and coordination difficulties. MRI confirms diagnosis; management is supportive unless hydrocephalus requires surgical intervention.

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is E. This patient has findings consistent with S1 radiculopathy, a common result of herniation of the L5–S1 disc compressing the S1 nerve root. Key localizing features include pain radiating down the posterior leg, sensory loss over the lateral foot, weakness in plantarflexion (gastrocnemius), and loss of the Achilles reflex, which is mediated by S1. A positive straight leg raise test supports nerve root irritation. The distribution of radiculopathy symptoms follows a dermatomal and myotomal pattern. In contrast, L4 radiculopathy affects the medial leg and patellar reflex, and L5 affects dorsiflexion and the great toe. Recognizing reflex associations is high-yield: patellar reflex = L4, Achilles reflex = S1. Management typically includes conservative therapy; red flag signs (e.g., cauda equina) would warrant urgent imaging. A 42-year-old man comes to the clinic with 3 weeks of progressive lower back pain radiating down the back of his left leg to the foot. He describes the pain as sharp and shooting, worsened by sitting and improved by lying flat. He denies bowel or bladder symptoms. Neurologic exam shows decreased sensation over the lateral aspect of the foot, reduced strength in plantarflexion, and an absent Achilles reflex on the left. Dorsiflexion and knee extension are intact. Straight leg raise is positive on the left side. Which of the following nerve roots is most likely affected?

stem: Subject System Topic
answer_text: L5

options.A: L2
options.B: L3
options.E: S1
options.F: S2
options.H: S4 Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: This root innervates the hip flexors (iliopsoas) and supplies sensation to the upper anterior thigh. It does not contribute to the Achilles reflex or plantarflexion. Pain would localize more proximally in the thigh. Choice B: Affects the quadriceps, impairing knee extension, and provides sensation to the anterior thigh and medial knee. Reflex changes would affect the patellar reflex, not Achilles. Plantarflexion and posterior leg pain would not occur. Choice C: L4 radiculopathy affects the medial leg, patellar reflex, and quadriceps. Weakness would involve knee extension and foot inversion, not plantarflexion or lateral foot sensation. Achilles reflex would remain intact. Choice D: L5 affects dorsiflexion, toe extension, and sensation over the dorsum of the foot, especially the big toe. Reflexes are often preserved, as L5 does not have a strong reflex association. The patient’s intact dorsiflexion rules this out. Choice F: This root helps innervate hamstrings and part of plantarflexion, but S1 is the dominant contributor. Isolated S2 injury would not match the classic Achilles reflex loss and lateral foot numbness seen here. Choice G: This root innervates pelvic floor structures and provides perineal sensation. Its involvement would result in saddle anesthesia or bowel/bladder dysfunction, which is not present here. Choice H: Like S3, this contributes to pelvic autonomic function and anal sphincter control. There is no urinary or perianal dysfunction, making this an incorrect choice. Bottom line: S1 radiculopathy causes loss of Achilles reflex, plantarflexion weakness, and sensory loss over the lateral foot, typically due to L5–S1 disc herniation. HY dermatomes and reflexes: C5 = biceps jerk; C7 = triceps reflex; T4 = nipples; T10 = umbilicus; L1 = groin; L3/4 = knee jerk(Patellar reflex); S1 = ankle jerk + sole of foot(Achilles reflex). stem: Choice B: Huntington disease is an autosomal dominant neurodegenerative disorder caused by a trinucleotide (CAG) repeat expansion in the HTT gene on chromosome 4. It presents with a triad of progressive choreiform movements, psychiatric disturbances (such as depression or irritability), and cognitive decline. Neuropathologically, there is degeneration of the caudate nucleus and putamen, with accompanying cortical atrophy. The disease typically manifests in mid-adulthood and worsens over 15–20 years. Huntington disease results from toxic gain-of-function of the huntingtin protein, not from iron deficiency or dopamine biosynthesis dysfunction. While both conditions may involve the basal ganglia, the movement features of Huntington disease are involuntary and hyperkinetic, unlike the voluntary leg movement behaviors seen in RLS. There is no increased risk of Huntington disease in patients with restless legs syndrome. Choice C: Multiple sclerosis (MS) is a chronic autoimmune demyelinating disorder of the central nervous system that primarily affects young adults, particularly women. It presents with neurologic deficits separated in time and space, such as optic neuritis, sensory loss, motor weakness, or cerebellar signs. MRI typically reveals periventricular white matter plaques, and CSF analysis may show oligoclonal bands. MS results from autoreactive T cells attacking myelin and is mediated by inflammatory cytokines. Although MS can involve various central pathways, including those responsible for movement and sensation, it does not impair dopamine synthesis or iron-dependent neurotransmission. RLS

subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is E. This patient presents with a classic constellation of symptoms suggesting Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS), an autoimmune disorder that often occurs as a paraneoplastic syndrome in patients with small cell lung carcinoma (SCLC). In LEMS, autoantibodies target presynaptic P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channels on motor nerve terminals, which impairs calcium influx required for acetylcholine (ACh) vesicle fusion and release into the neuromuscular junction, making it a type 2 hypersensitivity reaction. As a result, there is insufficient ACh release per nerve impulse, leading to reduced activation of postsynaptic nicotinic receptors and poor baseline muscle contraction. This reduction in neurotransmitter release explains the diminished strength and reflexes at rest. A 56-year-old woman presents to the clinic with progressive difficulty rising from A key electrophysiologic feature is the incremental improvement in muscle response with repetitive stimulation or exercise, due to calcium accumulation in the presynaptic terminal overcoming the blockade. This distinguishes LEMS from myasthenia gravis (MG), where the defect is postsynaptic and repeated stimulation causes a decremental response. chairs, climbing stairs, and lifting objects overhead for the past 3 weeks. She denies Reflex facilitation (improvement of deep tendon reflexes after exertion) is also characteristic of LEMS and not seen in MG. trauma, sensory symptoms, or prior neurologic disease but reports a long smoking history of one pack daily for 30 years. On examination, she has symmetric proximal muscle weakness affecting the shoulders and hips. Her deep tendon reflexes are diminished but improve after brief exercise. Repeated muscle contractions during strength testing lead to noticeable improvement in power. Sensory exam is normal. Laboratory tests show mild anemia but no electrolyte disturbances. A chest x-ray is shown below. Electrophysiologic studies demonstrate a reduced baseline muscle response that progressively improves with high-frequency repetitive nerve stimulation.

stem: Impairment of which of the following is the most likely underlying pathophysiologic mechanism? Left hilar mass, likely SCLC given the clinical picture of LEMS. Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: Acetylcholinesterase activity is normal in LEMS; this enzyme degrades ACh in the synaptic cleft after it binds receptors. While acetylcholinesterase inhibitors (e.g., pyridostigmine) are used to treat MG by prolonging ACh action, they are less effective in LEMS because the underlying problem is reduced ACh release, not excessive breakdown.

options.A: Acetylcholinesterase activity There is no increase in acetylcholinesterase activity in this patient’s condition. The synaptic cleft contains normal levels of enzyme, and turnover of ACh is not the primary issue. The core pathophysiologic defect is insufficient presynaptic ACh release. Choice B: Binding of acetylcholine at the postsynaptic membrane is impaired in MG due to autoantibodies against nicotinic ACh receptors. In LEMS, the postsynaptic receptors are structurally normal but are exposed to reduced ACh concentrations because of defective presynaptic release. Postsynaptic receptor density and function are preserved in this
options.B: Binding of acetylcholine at the postsynaptic membrane patient’s condition. The clinical improvement seen with exercise suggests an upstream defect that can be overcome by increased ACh availability, not impaired receptor binding. Therefore, impaired receptor binding is not the mechanism here. Choice C: Direct depolarization of muscle fibers by calcium does not physiologically occur; muscle depolarization results from sodium influx following ACh receptor activation. Calcium influx in muscle occurs later, as part of excitation-contraction coupling after depolarization is achieved. In LEMS, the problem lies before depolarization, at the level of
options.C: Direct depolarization of muscle fibers by calcium impaired neurotransmitter release from the nerve terminal. The muscle membrane remains electrically normal and capable of depolarization if stimulated appropriately. Calcium itself is not responsible for depolarizing muscle fibers at baseline.
options.D: Postsynaptic membrane potential Choice D: Postsynaptic membrane potential remains normal in LEMS because the postsynaptic muscle membrane is structurally and electrically intact. The abnormality lies upstream, at the presynaptic nerve terminal where reduced ACh release limits activation of postsynaptic receptors. The postsynaptic membrane would depolarize normally if sufficient neurotransmitter were delivered. This distinguishes LEMS from primary myopathies, which may alter muscle membrane properties directly. In this patient, postsynaptic excitability is intact but under-stimulated due to reduced presynaptic input.
options.E: Release of acetylcholine into the myoneural junction Bottom line: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome is caused by autoantibody-mediated destruction of presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing calcium influx and impairing acetylcholine release. This presynaptic defect leads to muscle weakness that improves with repeated stimulation and is associated with malignancies such as small cell lung carcinoma. stem: neurologic deficits or ischemic symptoms rather than isolated abduction palsy. Carotid artery involvement can also cause pulsatile exophthalmos, but not isolated cranial nerve VI palsy. Which labeled structure most likely explains the impaired abduction in this patient? Choice D: The oculomotor nerve (CN III) controls most extraocular muscles except the lateral rectus (abduction, CN VI) and superior oblique (depression/intorsion, CN IV). Injury to CN III presents with "down and out" eye deviation, ptosis, and possibly mydriasis if parasympathetic fibers are involved. In cavernous sinus thrombosis, CN III can be affected, but isolated impaired abduction is specifically due to CN VI involvement. Oculomotor nerve palsy may also present with diplopia and loss of accommodation. Choice E: The trochlear nerve innervates the superior oblique muscle, which primarily controls downward and inward gaze, not lateral movement. Trochlear nerve dysfunction causes vertical diplopia and difficulty looking down, such as when reading or walking downstairs. It does not explain impaired eye abduction observed in this patient. Choice F: The ophthalmic branch of the trigeminal nerve (V1) provides sensory innervation to the forehead, upper eyelid, and cornea. Involvement of V1 in cavernous sinus thrombosis can cause hypoesthesia or loss of corneal reflex, as seen in this patient. However, V1 does not control extraocular muscle movement; thus, its involvement does not explain the impaired abduction. Trigeminal nerve deficits may also predispose to neurotrophic keratitis due to corneal anesthesia.

stem: Injury or compression to which of the following spinal nerve roots is most likely responsible for this patient’s symptoms?
answer_text: S1

options.A: L1
options.B: L3
options.E: T12 Wrong answer explanations: Choice B: L3 contributes to the anterior thigh sensation and knee extension via the femoral nerve. L3 radiculopathy typically causes weakness in quadriceps strength and decreased patellar reflexes, along with pain radiating down the mid-thigh, not isolated groin symptoms. Choice C: L4 primarily innervates the medial leg and foot and contributes to the quadriceps and tibialis anterior muscles. Patients with L4 radiculopathy may have difficulty with knee extension or foot dorsiflexion and often experience shin and inner ankle numbness, which are not present here. Choice D: S1 affects the posterolateral leg and lateral foot, with motor effects including plantarflexion weakness and absent ankle reflexes. It does not affect the groin, and there is no evidence of distal leg involvement or gait instability in this case. Choice E: T12 overlaps slightly with L1 in the lower abdominal wall but does not contribute significantly to the inguinal or upper thigh sensory region. T12 lesions are more likely to cause flank or lower abdominal symptoms, not isolated groin numbness with hip flexion weakness. Bottom line: L1 radiculopathy presents with groin numbness and sometimes mild hip flexion weakness. It is best localized by altered sensation in the inguinal region, and often occurs from foraminal narrowing or degenerative spine disease affecting upper lumbar roots. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is D. Parkinson disease pathophysiology centers on degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta (label D), part of the midbrain. These neurons project to the striatum and normally modulate motor control by facilitating activity of the direct pathway (which promotes movement) and inhibiting the indirect pathway (which suppresses movement). Loss of dopamine leads to overactivity of the indirect pathway and underactivity of the direct pathway, resulting in reduced thalamocortical stimulation and the hallmark hypokinetic movement disorder of Parkinson disease. This progressive neurodegeneration disrupts basal ganglia circuitry, impairing the initiation and smooth execution of voluntary movements. Classic microscopic findings include Lewy bodies composed of α-synuclein aggregates within surviving neurons. The classic symptoms of Parkinson disease are resting tremors, cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia/akinesia, micrographia, shuffling, short steppage gait and postural instability. The primary management goal in Parkinson disease is symptomatic relief by restoring dopaminergic tone to the striatum. First-line pharmacologic treatment involves levodopa, a dopamine precursor that crosses the blood-brain barrier, combined with carbidopa to inhibit peripheral metabolism and enhance central bioavailability. Dopamine agonists (e.g., pramipexole), MAO-B inhibitors (e.g., selegiline), and COMT inhibitors (e.g., entacapone) may also be used, especially in younger patients or as adjuncts. In advanced disease refractory to medical therapy, deep brain stimulation targeting the subthalamic nucleus or globus pallidus internus may reduce motor fluctuations and improve quality of life. While treatment improves symptoms, disease progression continues, and patients often develop significant disability over time. A 71-year-old man is brought to the clinic by his daughter due to tremor and difficulty walking over the past year. She reports that he has developed a slow, shuffling gait and often freezes mid-step. He has reduced facial expression and soft, monotonous speech. The tremor is most prominent at rest and diminishes with voluntary movement. Examination reveals bradykinesia, cogwheel rigidity, and stooped posture. There is no sensory loss, normal reflexes, and no signs of stroke. The patient is independent in daily living activities but his gait is increasingly unsteady. MRI of the brain is performed; findings are shown below.

stem: Which labeled structure is most likely affected?
answer_text: C Wrong answer explanations:

options.A: A
options.B: B Choice A = Optic nerve; B = medial orbital gyrus; C = parietal lobe; D = midbrain; E= cerebellar vermis; F = Occipital pole (of occipital lobe).
options.D: D Choice A: The optic nerve transmits visual signals to the lateral geniculate body and damage here causes monocular vision loss. It has no direct role in motor modulation and a lesion here would result in painless visual impairment rather than resting tremor or bradykinesia. Diseases like optic neuritis or ischemic optic neuropathy affect this structure,
options.E: E presenting with afferent pupillary defect or reduced visual acuity. The optic nerve does not contribute to basal ganglia-thalamocortical circuits and cannot explain the parkinsonian syndrome seen in this patient. Visual field deficits or blurred vision would suggest pathology here. Choice B: The medial orbital gyrus is part of the prefrontal cortex involved in social cognition and emotional regulation. Injury here leads to behavioral disinhibition, emotional lability, or impaired judgment rather than movement disorders. Damage to this gyrus would not cause resting tremor, bradykinesia, or cogwheel rigidity. Frontal lobe syndromes
options.F: F typically present with impaired executive function and inappropriate social behavior. The basal ganglia circuitry implicated in Parkinson disease does not pass through this cortical area. Choice C: The parietal lobe is primarily responsible for processing sensory input and spatial orientation. Lesions here cause sensory neglect, astereognosis, or visuospatial deficits rather than motor slowing or tremor. A lesion of the parietal cortex would not disrupt dopaminergic signaling from the substantia nigra. Clinical presentations here often involve contralateral hemispatial neglect, particularly with right parietal damage. Parkinsonian motor findings are not attributable to parietal cortex injury. Choice E: The cerebellar vermis coordinates axial and truncal movements and maintains balance. Damage here results in truncal ataxia, wide-based gait, and difficulty maintaining upright posture, but not rigidity or tremor at rest. Alcoholic cerebellar degeneration preferentially affects the vermis, causing swaying gait without bradykinesia or resting tremor. Lesions in the vermis impair equilibrium and postural adjustment, distinct from basal ganglia dysfunction. This structure is not involved in dopaminergic modulation of movement. Choice F: The occipital lobe contains the primary visual cortex, which processes visual information from the contralateral hemifield. Lesions here cause contralateral homonymous hemianopia but do not affect voluntary motor control. Injury to the occipital cortex would not produce resting tremor, cogwheel rigidity, or bradykinesia. Visual hallucinations and cortical blindness are features of occipital pathology but are not present in Parkinson disease. The occipital lobe does not participate in basal ganglia-thalamocortical pathways. Bottom line: Parkinson disease results from degeneration of dopaminergic neurons in the substantia nigra pars compacta, leading to loss of modulation of the basal ganglia motor pathways and classic features of bradykinesia, rigidity, and resting tremor. Careful neuroanatomic localization distinguishes substantia nigra pathology from optic nerve, prefrontal cortex, parietal lobe, cerebellar vermis, or occipital lobe lesions, each of which has distinct clinical presentations. subject: General
explanation: The correct answer is C. The spinal accessory nerve innervates the sternocleidomastoid and trapezius muscles, which control head rotation and shoulder elevation. This nerve exits the skull via the jugular foramen along with CN IX and X. A lesion at the jugular foramen causes dysfunction of all three nerves: CN IX (gag reflex), CN X (palate elevation and voice), and CN XI (shoulder and head movement). Difficulty turning the head to the left implies weakness of the right sternocleidomastoid (ipsilateral), confirming right-sided CN XI involvement. A 65-year-old man presents with progressive hoarseness, dysphagia, and right shoulder weakness. He also reports difficulty turning his head to the left. Neurologic examination reveals decreased elevation of the right soft palate, uvula deviation to the left, and loss of the gag reflex on the right. MRI shows a mass involving the right jugular foramen. Which of the following cranial nerves is most likely responsible for his shoulder weakness?

stem: Subject System Topic
answer_text: Hypoglossal nerve (CN XII)

options.A: Glossopharyngeal nerve (CN IX)
options.B: Hypoglossal nerve (CN XII)
options.E: Vagus nerve (CN X) Wrong answer explanations: Choice A: CN IX mediates the afferent limb of the gag reflex but does not innervate muscles responsible for head rotation or shoulder elevation. Choice B: CN XII controls tongue movement and would present with tongue deviation, not shoulder weakness. Choice D: CN V is responsible for facial sensation and mastication. It does not contribute to head or shoulder movement. Choice E: CN X innervates the soft palate and larynx, which explains the hoarseness and uvula deviation, but not shoulder weakness. Bottom line: Jugular foramen syndrome affects CN IX, X, and XI. CN XI lesion results in ipsilateral weakness of head rotation and shoulder elevation.
